paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 1: All About Me and My Family
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar lessons
This unit has six small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Present Continuous  (what is happening now)
What it means and why it matters
We use the present continuous to talk about actions that are happening right now. It helps us say exactly what people are doing at this moment, like “The student is showing respect.”
How to form and use it
Use two parts: the correct form of be (am / is / are) + the verb with -ing.
I am + verb-ing:  “I am learning English.”
He / She / It is + verb-ing:  “My family is talking about safety.”
We / You / They are + verb-ing:  “They are helping their parents.”
Spelling tip: if a verb ends in e, drop the e (make → making). If a short verb ends in one vowel and one consonant, double the consonant (run → running).
A common mistake
Mistake: “They helping their parents.”  ✗
Fix: don't forget the be word: “They are helping their parents.”  ✓
Memory tip
If you can say “right now” after the sentence, use the present continuous. “She is reading — right now!”
Practice 1
Write the correct form: use am / is / are and add -ing to the verb in brackets.
The children ______ (play) outside.
She ______ (eat) her lunch.
We ______ (learn) about health.
He ______ (do) his homework.
My family ______ (talk) about safety.
Lesson 2:  Wh- Questions  (asking for information)
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions begin with who, what, where, when, why, and how. Good learners ask questions to find out more about people and the world.
How to form and use it
Who → a person:  “Who is your best friend?”
What → a thing or idea:  “What is your duty at school?”
Where → a place:  “Where do you play after school?”
When → a time:  “When do you have lunch?”
Why → a reason:  “Why do you like reading?”
How → the way:  “How do you get to school?”
Always finish a question with a question mark ( ? ).
A common mistake
Mistake: “What your name?”  ✗   (the verb is missing)
Fix: “What is your name?”  ✓
Memory tip
Wh- words are like keys that unlock answers. Pick the right key: When = time, Where = place, Who = person.
Practice 2
Write the correct Wh- word for each question.
______ do you have lunch? (a time)
______ is your favourite subject? (a thing)
______ is your best friend? (a person)
______ do you get to school? (the way)
Lesson 3:  Giving Instructions  (imperatives)
What it means and why it matters
When we tell someone what to do, we use the imperative. It gives a clear instruction or rule, like “Respect your parents” or “Behave well in public.”
How to form and use it
Start with the base verb — no ending:  “Listen to your teacher.”
You do not need to say “you”. It is already understood:  “Behave well” means “You behave well.”
Add please to be polite:  “Please help your friends.”
To say NOT to do something, use don't:  “Don't be rude.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You respects your parents.”  ✗
Fix: no “you”, and use the base verb: “Respect your parents.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the word command. An imperative gives one clear command to follow — short and strong.
Practice 3
Complete each instruction with the base verb in brackets. Add “Don't” where it says (not).
______ (listen) to your teacher.
______ (not / disturb) others while they are working.
______ (help) your classmates.
______ (be) kind to everyone.
Lesson 4:  Action Verbs  (doing words)
What it means and why it matters
Action verbs tell us what someone is doing, like help, learn, run, and play. They make your sentences come alive: instead of “They are,” you can say “They are helping their parents.”
How to form and use it
Find the subject (who is doing it):  “She is learning about health.”
Add the doing word (the action verb):  learning, helping, running.
For he / she / it in the simple present, add -s:  “He runs fast.”  “She helps her mum.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He run fast.”  ✗
Fix: for he / she / it, add -s to the verb: “He runs fast.”  ✓
Memory tip
Action = Doing. If someone is doing something, the word for it is an action verb.
Practice 4
Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.
She ______ (play) the drum beautifully.
They ______ (help) their friends every day.
I ______ (learn) new words every day.
He ______ (run) fast in the race.
Lesson 5:  Expressing Likes and Dislikes
What it means and why it matters
We talk about what we enjoy and what we do not enjoy. This helps us share our feelings, like “I like being with my family” or “He doesn't like public speaking.”
How to form and use it
Use like with I, you, we, they. Use likes with he, she, it.
“I like helping my parents.”
“She likes reading books.”
To say you do NOT enjoy something, use don't (I / you / we / they) or doesn't (he / she / it).
“We don't like loud noise.”
“He doesn't like getting up late.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He like football.”  ✗
Fix: add the “s” for he / she / it: “He likes football.”  ✓
Memory tip
I / you / we / they → like.   He / she / it → likes.   Don't want it? Put “not” after do or does.
Practice 5
Write like, likes, don't, or doesn't.
I ______ eating dates in the evening.
She ______ going to the park with her family.
They ______ (negative) like loud shouting in public.
He ______ (negative) like reading in a noisy room.
Lesson 6:  The Conjunction “and”
What it means and why it matters
A conjunction is a joining word. And joins two ideas or things together, so we can say two things at once, like “Honour and duty are both important.”
How to form and use it
Join two words:  “Health and safety go together.”
Join two ideas:  “I help my mum and I listen to my teacher.”
In a list of three, put a comma, then and:  “I like reading, drawing, and football.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I like apples I like oranges.”  ✗  (two ideas, no joining word)
Fix: join them with and: “I like apples and I like oranges.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “and” as a bridge between two islands. Each island is one idea; the bridge joins them.
Practice 6
Join the ideas with “and”.
I respect my parents ______ my teachers.
We must be kind ______ safe at school.
My family talks ______ laughs at dinner.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Continuous)
1. are playing   2. is eating   3. are learning   4. is doing   5. is talking
Practice 2 (Wh- Questions)
1. When   2. What   3. Who   4. How
Practice 3 (Imperatives)
1. Listen   2. Don't disturb   3. Help   4. Be
Practice 4 (Action Verbs)
1. plays   2. help   3. learn   4. runs
Practice 5 (Likes and Dislikes)
1. like   2. likes   3. don't   4. doesn't
Practice 6 (Conjunction “and”)
1. and   2. and   3. and

paragraphs: Unit 1: All About Me and My Family
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to your first unit of Year 3 English! In this unit you will learn how to talk about yourself and your family, and about the good values that hold a family and a school together — respect, honour, and duty. You will learn how to behave well in public and in private, and how to talk about health and safety. By the end, you will be ready to introduce your family and share what matters to you, just like Junior does in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Talk about the people in your family and what you do together.
Use new words like respect, honour, duty, health, and safety.
Say what is happening now, using the present continuous (“I am helping.”).
Ask Wh- questions to find out about other people.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Behave well.”).
Say what you like and don't like, and join ideas with and.
Explain how to behave in public and in private places.
Write simple sentences, questions, a short play, a letter, and instructions.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.
Do the six lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story file, “Junior's Big Day”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — My Family
Read this short text:

My name is Layla. I live with my family in a small house. My parents are my mum and my dad. I have one brother and one grandmother. We eat dinner together every night. My family helps me, and I help them. It is my duty to keep my room clean and to be kind. I love my family very much.
After reading, answer:
Who lives in Layla's family? Name every person.
What does Layla's family do together every night?
What is Layla's duty at home?
Now tell your AI tutor about the people in your own family.
Reading 2 — Good Behaviour at School
Read this short text:

At school, we show respect to our teacher and to our friends. We listen when other people are talking. In a public place, like the lunch area, we sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly. Good behaviour keeps everyone safe and happy. When we honour our teachers and help the younger students, our whole school feels like one big family.
After reading, answer:
Name two ways we show respect at school.
How should we behave in a public place, like the lunch area?
Why is good behaviour important?
Listening 1 — Junior Talks About His Family
Listen to Junior. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“Hello! My name is Junior. I live with my parents, my two sisters, and my grandmother. My parents are my role models. They teach me to help others and to be polite. It is my duty to honour them and to be a good student.”
Who are Junior's role models? What do they teach him?
What does Junior say his duty is?
Does Junior say “he” or “she” when he talks about his grandmother? Why? (See Grammar Lesson 4.)
Listening 2 — Public and Private
Listen carefully:
“In private, at home with your family, you can play and be loud. In public, at school or in the market, you stay calm and speak softly. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”
Where can you be loud — in public, or in private?
How should you behave in public?
Give one example of a public place and one example of a private place.
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Introduce Your Family
Get ready to say:
“In my family, there is ______, ______, and me.”
“My ______ helps me to ______.”
“Together, my family likes to ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the people clearly? Did you speak loudly enough?
Speaking 2 — What Do You Like?
“I like ______, and I like ______.”
“I don't like ______.”
“My best friend likes ______.”  (Remember: he/she likes — with an “s”!)
Speaking Game — Ask Me a Question
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person asks a Wh- question: “Who is in your family?” or “What is your duty at home?”
The other person answers in a full sentence.
Then swap turns. Try to use who, what, where, when, why, and how.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
I like helping my ______.
In my family, I ______.
She is learning about ______.
My duty at home is to ______.
Writing 2 — Write Some Questions
Write a Wh- question you could ask a new friend about each of these:
their family  →  “What ______?”
how they show respect at school  →  “How ______?”
what they like to do  →  “What ______?”
Writing 3 — A Short Play Script
Write a tiny play (four lines) where a junior student teaches a senior student about duty, like Junior does in the story. Use this shape:
Writing 4 — A Letter to a New Friend
Write a short letter to a new friend. Tell them about your family and about one value that matters to you, such as respect or honour. Use this shape:
Dear ______, / My name is ______. In my family there is ______. I think ______ is important because ______. Your friend, ______
Writing 5 — Instructions
Write three instructions (imperatives) for one of these titles: “How to behave in public” OR “How to stay safe at school”. Start each line with a base verb, like “Speak softly.”
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False (About Values)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
We show respect by shouting over other people.
A parent is a mother or a father.
In a public place, we should behave well and speak softly.
A duty is something you must do.
Answers: 1. False (we listen quietly)  2. True  3. True  4. True
Activity 2 — Match the Word to Its Meaning
Match each word to its meaning.
honour   →   (a) being safe and not in danger
safety   →   (b) something you must do
duty     →   (c) to show someone great respect
Answers: 1 → c,  2 → a,  3 → b
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
Use like, likes, public, or private.
The park is a ______ place where everyone can play.
My diary is ______; it is just for me.
I ______ helping my family.
She ______ reading books at night.
Answers: 1. public  2. private  3. like  4. likes
Activity 4 — Present Continuous
Write what is happening now. Use is / are + verb-ing.
The students ______ (practise) their play.
My family ______ (talk) about safety.
He ______ (help) the younger student.
Answers: 1. are practising  2. is talking  3. is helping
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about duty and respect?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can talk about the people in my family.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use words like respect, honour, and duty.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what is happening now (present continuous).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask Wh- questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give instructions using imperatives.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what I like and don't like, and join ideas with “and”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain how to behave in public and in private.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 2
Our next unit is Unit 2: “School and Learning”.
Make sure you know all the Unit 1 words on your Word Wall.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 2 about school and learning? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 1: All About Me and My Family
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Junior's Big Day”
Junior's Big Day
Junior was not his real name. His real name was Abdullahi. But everyone at school called him Junior, because his father was a teacher and everyone gave the family great respect.
At home, Junior lived with his big, happy family. His parents, his two sisters, and his grandmother all lived together in a small house near the town centre.
One morning, Junior was getting ready for school. His mother said, “Junior, remember to behave in public. Greet your teacher and listen well.”
“I will, Mama,” said Junior. “I am so excited! Today our class is doing a play about health and safety.”
His little sister Rahma asked, “What is the play about?”
“It is about staying safe at school and showing respect,” Junior explained.
At school, the students were busy. Some were practising their lines. Some were fixing the props with paper and paint. Junior put on a yellow vest and a hat he had made from old newspaper.
He whispered to his best friend, Abdi, “What is your duty in the play?”
“I am playing the senior student,” said Abdi. “You have to teach me how to act in public and private places.”
“Oh yes!” said Junior. “That is my job!”
The teacher clapped her hands. “Alright, everyone. We are starting now.” The classroom became quiet, and Junior stepped forward.
“Hello, Senior Student!” he said in a big voice. “This is how you behave in the lunch area. Sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly.”
“Why should I do that?” asked Abdi, pretending to be rude.
Junior replied, “Because we must show honour and respect. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”
Then another student walked in and shouted loudly. Junior smiled and said, “In private, you can be loud with your family. But in public, we stay calm.” The whole class laughed. They loved the play.
After the play, the teacher smiled. “Who are your role models?” she asked the class.
Junior raised his hand. “My parents,” he said. “They teach me to help others and to be polite. I want to honour them.”
The teacher nodded. “That is a wonderful answer, Junior.”
Later that day, Junior saw a junior student drop her school bag near the gate. Her books spilled onto the ground.
He ran over at once. “Let me help you,” he said kindly.
“Thank you!” said the girl, smiling. “You are very respectful.”  Junior smiled back. “I am trying to be.”
On the way home, Junior and Abdi walked together. “I liked your line in the play,” said Abdi. “‘Behave well, and keep others safe.’ That was clever.”
Junior grinned. “We should make posters for school,” he said. “Like ‘Be safe in public’ or ‘Use kind words.’”
“That is a great idea,” said Abdi. “Let us ask the teacher tomorrow.”
That evening at home, Junior was writing in his notebook. His grandmother came in. “What are you doing?” she asked.
“I am writing about my duty,” he said. “To help others and to be a good student.” He showed her what he had written:
“At school, I behave with respect. In public, I speak softly. In private, I relax with my family. My duty is to learn, to help, and to stay healthy.”
His mother peeked into the room. “Time for bed,” she said gently.
“Okay, Mama,” said Junior. “Tomorrow I will keep being my best.”
“Good,” she said. “Because every day is a new chance to do your duty.”

Junior closed his eyes and smiled. He liked his nickname now. It reminded him that he was still learning, but growing up with honour. And maybe one day, he would be a senior student that others could look up to.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What is Junior's real name? Why does everyone call him Junior?
Who lives in Junior's family?
What was the class play about?
In the play, how should you behave in public? How can you behave in private?
Who are Junior's role models, and why?
What kind thing did Junior do for the junior student at the gate?
Think and talk
How does Junior show respect at home and at school? Give one example of each.
The teacher asked, “Who are your role models?” Who is a role model for you, and why?
Junior said, “In public, we stay calm.” Why is it good to behave well in public places?
What is one duty you have in your family? How does it help the people you love?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 1 words and say the sentence they are in:
family
parents
behave
respect
public
private
duty
honour
senior
junior
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about your own family. Start with: “In my family, I ______.”
Like Junior, write your own short list of duties. Start each line with “My duty is to ______.”
Draw Junior helping the girl at the gate. Write one sentence under your picture about showing respect.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 2: School and Learning
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  The Simple Past Tense
What it means and why it matters
We use the simple past tense to talk about things that already happened — yesterday, last week, or a long time ago. It helps us tell stories and say what we did.
How to form it
Most verbs add –ed:  study → studied,  play → played,  jump → jumped.
Some verbs are special (irregular):  go → went,  have → had,  eat → ate.
Time words help, like yesterday, last week, or two days ago.
Example: “The student studied for the grammar contest.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He goed to the park.”  ✗
Fix: “go” is a special verb, so we say went: “He went to the park.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the past tense as a time capsule! When you talk about something that happened before now, open the capsule and use the past.
Practice 1
Write the past tense of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday, I ______ (visit) my grandmother.
They ______ (play) football last week.
We ______ (eat) rice for dinner.
She ______ (prepare) her report last night.
Lesson 2:  Yes/No Questions in the Past
What it means and why it matters
These questions help us ask if something happened in the past. The answer is “yes” or “no.” They make our conversations more interesting.
How to form it
With most verbs, use Did + subject + the base verb:  “Did you eat?”
For how someone or something was, use Was/Were + subject:  “Was she happy?”
Use was with I, he, she, it. Use were with you, we, they.
Example: “Did you forget your eraser?”  ·  “Was the author at the lesson?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Did he went to the store?”  ✗
Fix: after did, use the base verb (no –ed): “Did he go to the store?”  ✓
Memory tip
Did + subject + base verb (Did you eat?). Was/Were + subject (Was he happy? / Were they excited?).
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with Did, Was, or Were.
______ you see the film last week?
______ she at the contest?
______ they play outside yesterday?
______ it cold last night?
Lesson 3:  Wh– Questions in the Past
What it means and why it matters
Wh– questions start with words like what, where, when, who, why, and how. In the past, they help us find out about things that already happened.
How to form it
Use: Wh– word + did + subject + base verb.
Example: “What did you learn in mathematics?”
Example: “Where did the contest happen?”
Special case: when who is the subject, we do not use “did”:  “Who visited the library?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “What did you learned?”  ✗
Fix: after did, use the base verb: “What did you learn?”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the word DID as a helper. When you ask a past question, “Did” is there to help you find out what happened!
Practice 3
Complete each question with the correct Wh– word (What, When, or Where).
______ did you eat for lunch?
______ did the lesson start?
______ did you go on the school trip?
Lesson 4:  Using ‘from’ to Show Where Something Comes From
What it means and why it matters
We use the word from to show where something comes from — a book, a person, or a place. It helps us give clear information about the source of things.
How to form it
Use: subject + verb + from + the origin.
Example: “This topic is from our grammar book.”
You can also say where a person comes from: “I come from Somalia.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “This book is our grammar book.”  ✗ (the word from is missing)
Fix: “This idea is from our grammar book.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of from as a little bridge that connects a thing to the place it came from.
Practice 4
Fill in each blank with from.
This song is ______ a popular singer.
The homework is ______ our last lesson.
My friend is ______ Kenya.
Lesson 5:  Present Tense for Customs and Statements
What it means and why it matters
We use the present tense to talk about things that happen again and again, like habits and customs, or things that are always true.
How to form it
With I, you, we, they → use the base verb:  “They play.”  ·  “Teachers give lessons every morning.”
With he, she, it → add –s or –es:  “He plays.”  ·  “She goes to school.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He play football.”  ✗
Fix: with he, she, or it, add –s: “He plays football.”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember the little rhyme: “He, she, it — add an –s to it!”
Practice 5
Write the correct present-tense form of the verb in brackets.
My dad ______ (cook) breakfast every day.
The students ______ (read) books in class.
She ______ (play) with her friends on weekends.
They ______ (study) grammar every morning.
Lesson 6:  The Possessive ’s
What it means and why it matters
We use the possessive ’s to show that something belongs to someone. For example, “the teacher’s pen” means the pen belongs to the teacher. It makes it clear who owns what.
How to form it
For one owner, add ’s to the name:  student → student’s,  teacher → teacher’s.
Then put it before the thing: “the student’s report,” “the teacher’s lesson.”
If the owner is plural and ends in –s, just add an apostrophe: “the students’ reports.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The teachers lesson was about authors.”  ✗ (the apostrophe is missing)
Fix: “The teacher’s lesson was about authors.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of ’s as a little ownership sign. Find the owner, and stick the little ’s on!
Practice 6
Write the correct possessive form of the owner in brackets.
The ______ (cat) toy is missing.
The ______ (teacher) desk is tidy.
My ______ (sister) book is on the table.
The ______ (child) drawing is on the wall.
Lesson 7:  The Conjunction ‘because’
What it means and why it matters
The word because joins two parts of a sentence. It explains the reason why something happens or why we feel a certain way.
How to form it
Take a main idea and a reason. Join them with because.
Main idea: “I like the library.”  Reason: “It is quiet.”
Together: “I like the library because it is quiet.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I like the library it is quiet because.”  ✗
Fix: put because between the two ideas: “I like the library because it is quiet.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of because as a bridge between two ideas. If it explains “why,” you can use because.
Practice 7
Finish each sentence with your own reason, using because.
I love reading ______.
We went to the library ______.
I enjoy grammar ______.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Simple Past Tense)
1. visited   2. played   3. ate   4. prepared
Practice 2 (Yes/No Questions)
1. Did   2. Was   3. Did   4. Was
Practice 3 (Wh– Questions)
1. What   2. When   3. Where
Practice 4 (Using ‘from’)
1. from   2. from   3. from
Practice 5 (Present Tense)
1. cooks   2. read   3. plays   4. study
Practice 6 (Possessive ’s)
1. cat’s   2. teacher’s   3. sister’s   4. child’s
Practice 7 (because)
Answers will be different for everyone. Example: 1. I love reading because stories are fun. 2. We went to the library because we wanted to study. 3. I enjoy grammar because it helps me write well.

paragraphs: Unit 2: School and Learning
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 2! This unit is all about school and learning. You will talk about your lessons, your teacher, and the subjects you study, like mathematics and grammar. You will learn the words for the supplies you use every day, such as an eraser and your books. You will find out how to get ready for a contest, write a short report, and use the library well. Best of all, you will learn to talk about things that happened in the past — what you studied, where you went, and why you enjoyed it. By the end, you will be ready to cheer for the Grammar Champions in this unit's story!
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name the people, places, and supplies at school, using words like teacher, library, and eraser.
Talk about your school subjects, such as mathematics and grammar.
Tell about things that happened before, using the simple past tense (studied, prepared, went).
Ask past-tense questions, like “Did you study?” and “What did you learn?”
Show who owns something, using the possessive ’s (the teacher’s pen).
Give reasons using the word because.
Prepare for a contest and write a short report about a topic.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few school words each day.
Work through the Grammar file. There are seven short lessons — do one at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Grammar Champions,” and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — A Day at School
Read this short text:

My name is Yusuf. I go to school every morning. First, we have a mathematics lesson. I like numbers, but grammar is my favourite subject because it helps me write well. My teacher, Mr. Warsame, is kind. He helps us study new words. At break time, I go to the library to read. Education is important to my family. One day, I want to graduate and become a teacher too!
After reading, answer:
What is Yusuf's favourite subject? Why does he like it?
Where does Yusuf go at break time?
What does Yusuf want to be one day?
Which of your subjects do you like best? Why? (Use the word because.)
Reading 2 — Getting Ready for the Contest
Read this short text:

Last week, our class had a spelling contest. My group wanted to win, so we made a good plan. We went to the library. We studied our words twice each day. We checked every letter with a friend. On the day of the contest, we felt ready. We did not win first place, but we were proud. We learned that hard work and teamwork help us do our best.
After reading, answer:
What kind of contest did the class have?
Name two things the group did to prepare.
How did the group feel on the day of the contest?
Find two past-tense verbs in the text (words that end in -ed or tell about the past).
Listening 1 — In the Classroom
Listen to this classroom talk. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

Teacher: “Good morning, class. Today's lesson is about authors. Did you bring your books?” Aisha: “Yes, teacher! I brought my book and my eraser.” Teacher: “Very good, Aisha. What did you read at home?” Aisha: “I read a story about a clever camel. The author is very funny!”
What is today's lesson about?
What two things did Aisha bring to school?
What did Aisha read at home?
The teacher asks, “Did you bring your books?” Is this about now or the past? (See Grammar Lesson 2.)
Listening 2 — A Learning Song
Sing along:
“I studied, I studied, I studied today! I read my report and I put it away. In the library, quiet and bright, I learn something new — morning, noon, and night!”
What did the singer do today?
Where does the singer learn?
Can you find a past-tense word in the song?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Talk About Your School Day
Get ready to say three sentences about your day at school:
“Today I studied ______.”
“My favourite subject is ______ because ______.”
“At school I use a ______.” (name a supply)
Record yourself. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use the word because? Did you name a subject and a supply?
Speaking 2 — Ask and Answer About the Past
Practise asking a partner or your AI tutor these questions, then answer them:
“What did you study yesterday?”
“Did you go to the library this week?”
“Where did you do your homework?”
Speaking Game — Guess My Subject
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of a school subject, but do not say it.
Give a clue: “In this subject, we work with numbers.”
The other person guesses: “Is it mathematics?” Say “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — Sentences About School
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
Today the teacher gave a lesson about ______.
I like the library because ______.
Yesterday I studied ______.
This is my ______. (write a sentence using the possessive ’s, like “This is Aisha's eraser.”)
Writing 2 — A Short Report
Write a short report (3–4 sentences) about a topic you know well — maybe an animal, a game, or your town. Choose your topic, add some details, and end with one sentence about why you like it.
Then draw a small picture to go with your report.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Past Tense (Fill in the Blank)
Write the past tense of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday, I ______ (study) for the contest.
She ______ (prepare) her report last night.
We ______ (go) to the library on Monday.
They ______ (read) a book by a funny author.
Answers: 1. studied  2. prepared  3. went  4. read
Activity 2 — Word Match
Match each word to its meaning. Write the correct letter.
eraser
library
teacher
contest
a. a place with many books to read or borrow
b. a game or test to see who is best
c. a tool that cleans pencil marks
d. a person who helps us learn
Answers: 1. c  2. a  3. d  4. b
Activity 3 — Ask the Question (Wh- Questions in the Past)
Write the question for each answer.
______________________?  →  “I studied mathematics.”
______________________?  →  “The contest happened in our classroom.”
______________________?  →  “I went to the library because it is quiet.”
Answers: 1. What did you study?  2. Where did the contest happen?  3. Why did you go to the library? (answers may vary a little)
Activity 4 — Because Sentences
Join the two ideas with the word because.
I like grammar. It helps me write well.
We went to the library. We wanted to study.
Amina felt proud. Her group won the contest.
Example answer: 1. I like grammar because it helps me write well.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise the past tense: “Can you give me five verbs to change into the past tense?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my report and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more ‘because’ sentences to finish?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name school people, places, and supplies.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can talk about my school subjects.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use the simple past tense.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can ask past-tense questions (Did …? / What did …?).   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use the possessive ’s.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can give a reason with “because”.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a short report about a topic.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Getting Ready for Unit 3
Our next unit is Unit 3: Time and Daily Life. Get ready like this:
Make sure you know all the Unit 2 words on your Word Wall.
Start noticing the time of day when you do things — morning, afternoon, and evening.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 3? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning takes time and practice. Be patient, keep studying a little each day, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 2: School and Learning
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Grammar Champions”
The Grammar Champions
Amina sat quietly in her classroom at Kisii Central Primary School. She held her pencil in one hand and her eraser in the other. Her teacher had just said something exciting.
“Next Friday,” said Mr. Oloo, “we will have a grammar contest!”
The whole class cheered.
“You will work in groups,” he explained. “You will read, write, and even perform a short lesson. Start to prepare today!”
Amina joined her group with her friends Baraka and Joy. Their group name was “The Grammar Champions.”
“We need to choose a topic,” said Baraka.
“Let's pick something from the grammar book,” said Joy.
“What about prepositions?” asked Amina. “We can show how to use small words like ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
“I like it,” said Baraka. “It is simple, but important.”
The next day, the group met in the library. They wanted to study together and write their report.
Joy opened a book and smiled. “This author is funny,” she said. “He explains grammar with cartoons!”
Amina's eyes lit up. “That gives me an idea,” she said. “We can draw pictures too.”
Baraka brought a notebook full of details. “These are notes from my sister's class,” he said. “She already graduated from primary school.”
“She must be clever,” said Joy.
“She is,” said Baraka. “She loves education.”

For the next few days, the group worked hard. They practised their lesson in front of the class. They wrote their report about grammar rules. They checked every spelling word twice. They even collected classroom supplies like coloured chalk, tape, and posters.
Finally, it was contest day. The classroom had bright decorations and rows of chairs for visitors.
One of the judges asked, “Did you study well?”
“Yes, we did!” said Amina.
Another judge smiled. “What did you learn in mathematics today?”
“About shapes,” said Baraka. “But grammar is our focus today!”
When it was their turn, the Grammar Champions stood up tall.
Baraka began. “This topic is from our grammar book.”
Joy added, “We will show how to use the words ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
Amina stepped forward and pointed to the board. “The teacher's pen is ‘on’ the desk,” she said. “The bag is ‘under’ the table.”
Baraka drew a funny cartoon on the board. Everyone laughed and clapped.
After the show, the judges talked quietly. Then Mr. Oloo stood up and announced, “The winners are… The Grammar Champions!”
The class cheered again.
“We won!” whispered Amina. “All that preparing helped us.”
Later, the teacher asked, “Where did the contest happen?”
“In our own classroom,” Joy answered.
“And why did we win?” asked Baraka.
“Because we worked as a team,” said Amina. “And we gave a clear lesson with good details.”
At home, Amina told her mother the good news. “I feel so proud,” she said. “I want to learn even more grammar.”
Her mother hugged her. “That,” she said, “is the spirit of education.”
The next morning, Amina wrote in her journal:
I studied my grammar, I wrote a report,
I used my eraser to fix and to sort.
In the library, I read with my friends,
And now I have learned how good learning ends —
With joy, and with help, and a clever plan.
We're Grammar Champions. Yes, we can!
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What kind of contest did Mr. Oloo tell the class about?
What is the name of Amina's group?
What topic did the group choose for their lesson?
Where did the group go to study and write their report?
Why did the Grammar Champions win? Give two reasons.
Think and talk
How did Amina feel at the end of the story? Why?
The group won because they worked as a team. Why is teamwork helpful when you learn?
Amina says she wants to “learn even more grammar.” What is one thing you would like to learn more about?
How was the contest in the story the same as, or different from, an activity at your own school?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 2 words and say the sentence they are in:
contest
eraser
topic
library
study
report
author
details
graduate
supplies
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about a time you prepared for something at school. Start with: “First, I ______.”
Imagine you are in the Grammar Champions. Write one sentence about what you would do to help your team.
Draw Amina, Baraka, and Joy holding their prize. Write one sentence under your picture about how they feel.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 3: Time and Daily Life
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson at a time, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom of the file. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Do not rush; one lesson a day is enough.
Lesson 1:  Present Simple
What it means and why it matters

We use the Present Simple to talk about things that happen again and again, and about facts that are always true. It is the “routine tense”. When we say “She marks the calendar every morning,” we mean she does it regularly. This helps us describe our daily life clearly.
How to form it
Start with the subject: I, you, he, she, it, we, they.
Use the base verb (the verb with no “to”): mark, play, go.
For he, she, it, add s or es: “She marks the calendar.” “He watches TV.”
Negative: use do not / does not + base verb: “She does not play soccer.”
Question: start with Do / Does: “Does he go to school at 8 o’clock?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She mark the calendar.”  ✗
Fix: For she, add the “s”: “She marks the calendar.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the Present Simple as the routine tense. He, she and it never forget their “s”!
Practice 1
Fill in the blank with the Present Simple form of the verb in brackets.
My brother ______ (play) games every weekend.
The cat ______ (chase) the mouse in the garden.
We ______ (not eat) junk food.
______ (she / read) a book every night?
The train ______ (leave) at 9 o’clock.
Lesson 2:  Past Simple
What it means and why it matters

We use the Past Simple to talk about things that already happened at a certain time in the past. It helps us tell stories and share what we did. When we say “We went on vacation in August,” we are talking about the past.
How to form it
For most verbs, add -ed to the base verb: play → played, visit → visited.
Some verbs are irregular and change fully: go → went, see → saw (see Lesson 3).
Negative: use did not (didn’t) + base verb: “I didn’t see the calendar.”
Question: start with Did + subject + base verb: “Did you see the calendar?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She finish her homework yesterday.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ed for regular verbs: “She finished her homework yesterday.”  ✓
Memory tip
The Past Simple is a time machine. To travel back, add “-ed” to regular verbs — and learn the special irregular ones by heart.
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with the Past Simple form of the verb in brackets.
Last year, we ______ (go) to the beach.
I ______ (see) a good film last weekend.
They ______ (play) in the park yesterday.
She ______ (visit) her friend last month.
Lesson 3:  Irregular Past Verbs
What it means and why it matters
Most verbs add “-ed” in the past, but some special verbs change in their own way. These are irregular past verbs. We must learn them by heart, because they do not follow the “-ed” rule.
How to form it
Change the whole verb to its special past form. Some common ones:
go → went:  “They went on vacation.”
see → saw:  “She saw the calendar.”
eat → ate:  “He ate his lunch quickly.”
have → had:  “We had a great time.”
take → took:  “I took my dog for a walk.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “They goed to the store.”  ✗
Fix: “go” is irregular, so it becomes went: “They went to the store.”  ✓
Memory tip
Make flashcards: write the present verb on one side (go) and the past on the other (went). Practise with a friend or your AI tutor.
Practice 3
Fill in the blank with the correct irregular past verb from the word bank: went, saw, ate, had, took.
Yesterday, I ______ my homework out of my bag.
They ______ to the park last week.
She ______ a delicious sandwich for lunch.
We ______ a great time at the wedding.
He ______ the film last night.
Lesson 4:  Comparatives and Superlatives
What it means and why it matters

We use comparatives to compare two things (bigger, warmer). We use superlatives to talk about the best or the most of three or more things (the biggest, the warmest). This helps us describe and compare clearly.
How to form it
Short adjectives — comparative adds -er, superlative adds -est: warm → warmer → the warmest.
Adjectives ending in -y — change y to i: happy → happier → the happiest.
Long adjectives — use more and the most: beautiful → more beautiful → the most beautiful.
Irregular: good → better → the best;  bad → worse → the worst.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He is more tall than his brother.”  ✗
Fix: “tall” is short, so add “-er”: “He is taller than his brother.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think: “-er” is one step up (comparing two), and “-est” is the top of the steps (comparing many). “June is warmer than April; July is the warmest.”
Practice 4
Fill in the blank with the correct form of the adjective in brackets.
My sister is ______ (smart) than I am.
This puzzle is the ______ (hard) one I have ever tried.
A cheetah is ______ (fast) than a lion.
This is the ______ (funny) film I have seen all year.
Lesson 5:  Yes/No and Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters

We ask questions to find out things. Yes/No questions can be answered with yes or no (“Did you miss the lesson?”). Wh- questions ask for more information and begin with who, what, when, where, why or how (“When is your vacation?”).
How to form it
Yes/No: Helping verb + subject + main verb?  “Did you miss the lesson?”
Helping verbs include: do, does, did, is, are, was, were, can, will.
Wh-: Wh- word + helping verb + subject + main verb?  “When did you go?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Your vacation when is in September?”  ✗
Fix: Put the words in order — Wh- word first: “When is your vacation in September?”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember the pattern: for Yes/No questions, “Helping verb + subject + main verb?”. For Wh- questions, add the Wh- word at the very front.
Practice 5
Choose the correct word to complete each question.
______ (Do / Did) you enjoy the film? (it is over now)
______ (What / Where) is your favourite book?
______ (Can / Will) you help me with my project, please?
______ (Who / When) is your best friend?
Lesson 6:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are sentences that give a command, an instruction or a request. They tell someone to do something. We use them a lot when we explain how to do something — like how to make a calendar.
How to form it
Start with the base verb: Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.
No subject — we do not say “you”. Not “You clean your room”, just “Clean your room.”
Negative: add do not / don’t: “Don’t be late!”
Polite: add please: “Please listen carefully.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Preparing your calendar.”  ✗
Fix: Use the base verb, not the -ing form: “Prepare your calendar.”  ✓
Memory tip
Imperatives are action words. When you want someone to do something, just say the action — and remember, there is no “you” in an imperative.
Practice 6
Fill in the blank with the correct imperative (base) form of the verb in brackets.
______ your homework before dinner. (finish)
______ the plants every day. (water)
______ your shoes before you enter. (take off)
______ the table for lunch. (set)
______ your teacher when you have a question. (ask)
Lesson 7:  Pronouns
What it means and why it matters

Pronouns are small words we use instead of a noun, so we do not repeat the same name again and again. Instead of “Maria is an artist. Maria loves to paint,” we say “Maria is an artist. She loves to paint.” This makes our sentences smoother.
How to form it
I — for yourself: “I like reading.”
he — one boy or man;  she — one girl or woman.
it — a thing, animal or idea: “It is raining.”
we — you and others;  they — more than one person or thing.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Her is my friend.”  ✗
Fix: Use the subject pronoun at the start: “She is my friend.”  ✓
Memory tip
First find the noun, then swap in the matching pronoun: a girl → she, a thing → it, a group → they.
Practice 7
Choose the correct pronoun to complete each sentence.
______ am going to the market. (I / They)
Lisa is my best friend. ______ is very kind. (She / He)
The cat is sleeping. ______ looks so cute! (It / They)
Tom and I are friends. ______ like to play football. (We / He)
The girls are laughing. ______ are having fun. (They / She)
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Simple)
1. plays   2. chases   3. do not eat   4. Does she read   5. leaves
Practice 2 (Past Simple)
1. went   2. saw   3. played   4. visited
Practice 3 (Irregular Past Verbs)
1. took   2. went   3. ate   4. had   5. saw
Practice 4 (Comparatives & Superlatives)
1. smarter   2. hardest   3. faster   4. funniest
Practice 5 (Yes/No & Wh- Questions)
1. Did   2. What   3. Can   4. Who
Practice 6 (Imperatives)
1. Finish   2. Water   3. Take off   4. Set   5. Ask
Practice 7 (Pronouns)
1. I   2. She   3. It   4. We   5. They

paragraphs: Unit 3: Time and Daily Life
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will learn all about time and daily life. You will name the twelve months of the year and put them in order. You will talk about your daily routine — what you do at each hour of the day. You will learn to talk about the past (what already happened), the present (what happens every day), and the future (what will happen soon). By the end, you will be able to make your own calendar, just like Laila does in this unit's story, “The Calendar on the Wall”.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and spell the twelve months of the year, in order.
Talk about your daily routine using the hours of the day.
Use the Present Simple to talk about what you do every day.
Use the Past Simple and irregular verbs to talk about what already happened.
Talk about the future and about what will happen soon.
Compare things with -er and -est (comparatives and superlatives).
Ask and answer Yes/No and Wh- questions about time.
Give simple instructions using imperatives (Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.).
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few months and time words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one lesson a day.
Read the Story file, “The Calendar on the Wall”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — My Day, Hour by Hour
Read this short text about Yusuf's day:

My name is Yusuf. I have a busy day. At 6 o’clock, I wake up and pray. At 7, I eat breakfast with my family. At 8, I walk to school. We study for many hours. At 1 o’clock, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help my mother. In the evening, I read a book. Every day is a little different, but I always try to plan my time.
After reading, answer:
What does Yusuf do at 6 o’clock? What does he do at 1 o’clock?
How many meals does Yusuf talk about? When does he eat them?
What do you do at those same hours of your day?
Reading 2 — The Twelve Months
Read this text about the year:

A year has twelve months. The first month is January, and the last is December. Some months are hot and some are cool. Some months have rain and some are dry. In our school, the year begins in January and ends in November. Families use a calendar to remember special days, such as Eid, birthdays, and the days to plant and pick their crops.
After reading, answer:
How many months are there in a year? Name the first and the last.
In the text, when does the school year begin and end?
What special days does your family mark on the calendar?
Listening 1 — Making a Calendar
Listen to Laila talk about her calendar. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“I made a calendar for the whole year. In June, we have school games. In August, we go on vacation to visit my grandma. In October, we pick mangoes. I marked every special month!”
What does Laila do in June? What does she do in August?
Which month does Laila pick mangoes in?
Which verb tells us this is a routine that happens every year? (See Grammar Lesson 1.)
Listening 2 — The Months Song
Sing along, and count the months on your fingers:
“January, February, March and April, May and June, July, August, September, October, November, December — twelve months make one year!”
How many months make one year?
Which month comes after September? Which month comes before June?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — My Daily Routine
Get ready to tell your AI tutor about your day. Use the hours:
“In the morning, I ______.”
“In the evening, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say at least four things? Did you use the hours of the day? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Past, Present and Future
Say one sentence for each time:
Past: “Last vacation, I ______.” (use a past verb, like went or visited)
Present: “Every day, I ______.”
Future: “In the future, I want to ______.”
Speaking Game — When Is It?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person names something, such as a birthday, Eid, or the school games.
The other person asks: “When is it?”
Answer with a month: “It is in ______.” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — My Daily Routine (Information Report)
Write four or five sentences about what you do each day. Use the hours and the Present Simple:
At 6 o’clock, I ______.
In the evening, I ______.
Writing 2 — My Last Vacation (Summary)
Write three sentences about your last vacation. Use the Past Simple. Answer: When did it happen? Where did you go? What did you do?
Example: “Last August, we went to visit my grandma. We picked mangoes and played games. It was a happy vacation.”
Writing 3 — How to Make a Calendar (Instructions)
Write three instructions that tell someone how to make a calendar. Start each one with a command word (an imperative). See Grammar Lesson 6.
Example: “First, draw twelve squares. Next, write one month in each square. Then, mark your special days.”
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Put the Months in Order
Write these months in the correct order: May, January, March, April, February, June.
Answer: January, February, March, April, May, June.
Activity 2 — True or False (Time Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
There are 12 months in a year.
There are 60 minutes in one hour.
A century is 10 years.
September comes sooner than November.
Answers: 1. True   2. True   3. False (a century is 100 years)   4. True
Activity 3 — Past or Present?
Choose the correct verb.
Every morning, she ______ (marks / marked) the calendar.
Last week, we ______ (go / went) on vacation.
They ______ (play / played) football yesterday.
He ______ (walks / walked) to school every day.
Answers: 1. marks   2. went   3. played   4. walks
Activity 4 — Fill in the Blanks
Today's month is ______.
My favourite month is ______, because ______.
There are ______ months in a year.
In the future, I want to ______.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this month? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about the Past Simple?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name the twelve months in order.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about my daily routine using the hours.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use the Present Simple for things I do every day.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use the Past Simple to talk about what happened.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about the future and what will happen soon.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare things with -er and -est.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask and answer questions about time.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give simple instructions (imperatives).   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 4

Next you will study Unit 4: “Places and Community”. You will learn to describe the places around you — your home, the market, the mosque and the town — and talk about the people who help your community.
Make sure you know all the Unit 3 months and time words on your Word Wall.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 4? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 3: Time and Daily Life
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Calendar on the Wall”
The Calendar on the Wall
One bright Monday morning, Laila sat in class and looked at the big calendar on the wall. Her teacher had just asked a question.
“When does the school year begin?”
“In January,” said Laila.
“Good,” said the teacher. “And when does it end?”
“In November,” said another student.
“Correct. Now let us each make a calendar that shows what we do each month.”
Laila liked the idea. She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, February, March, and so on.
Then she thought about the future.
“In June, we have school games,” she wrote. “In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma.”
That night, at home, she showed the calendar to her grandmother.
“In October, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma, smiling. “And in December, the weather is hot.”
“What happened in the old days, Grandma?” asked Laila.
“Oh, a lot,” she said. “A century ago, people did not have printed calendars. They watched the sky, the moon, and the trees to know the time of year.”
Laila’s eyes grew wide. “A hundred years ago?”
“Yes,” Grandma laughed. “A century sounds long, but it goes faster than you think.”
The next day at school, the teacher asked, “Did you prepare your calendar?”
“Yes,” said Laila. “And I asked my Grandma about the past.”
“Great,” said the teacher. “Now add some details. What happens each hour of your day?”
Laila wrote: “At 6 o’clock, I wake up. At 7, I eat breakfast. At 8, I walk to school. At 1, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help with the chores.”
Then she thought about the future again. “When I grow up,” she wrote, “I want to be a teacher. I will help students plan their day and prepare for the next century.”
Later in the week, the school had a quiz.
“What month comes sooner — April or May?” asked the teacher.
“April,” said Laila. “It comes sooner.”
“And what will happen soon?” asked the teacher.
“Our vacation!” the class shouted together.
At the end of the week, parents visited the school. Laila showed her calendar to her mother.
“Wow,” said her mother. “You even marked the days we plant maize.”
Laila smiled. “I want to remember everything.”
Her mother added, “That is smart. The calendar helps us plan.”
On the last page of her book, Laila wrote a short poem:
The months go fast, the hours too.
The future is coming, it’s all brand new.
Look at the past, remember it well,
Time is a story we all must tell.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
In which month does the school year begin? In which month does it end?
What did the teacher ask each student to make?
What does Laila plan to do in June, and what in August?
What did people watch to tell the time of year a century ago, before printed calendars?
Write down three things Laila does at different hours of her day.
What does Laila want to be in the future? Why do you think she chose that?
Think and talk
Grandma said, “A century sounds long, but it goes faster than you think.” What do you think she meant?
Laila’s mother said, “The calendar helps us plan.” How does a calendar help your own family plan?
Which month is your favourite, and what happens in it where you live?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 3 words and say the sentence they are in:
calendar
January
November
future
vacation
century
hour
sooner
soon
Now You Try:  Writing
Make your own month calendar. For four months, write one thing you do in each month, just like Laila did. Start with: “In ______, I ______.”
Write about your daily routine. Use the hours of your day: “At 6 o’clock, I ______. At 7, I ______.”
Ask an older person in your family what life was like when they were young. Write two sentences about the past, using past-tense verbs.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 4: Places and Community
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. For each one, read what it means, look at the form and the examples, learn the common mistake (✗ wrong → ✓ right), remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
What it means and why it matters

An imperative is a special way to use a verb to give a command or an instruction. It tells someone to do something. We need imperatives to give clear directions, like “Exit the court quietly” or “Go to the hospital if you are sick.”
How to form it
Start with the base verb (the action word), with no “to”:  Go. / Stop. / Listen.
You do not say “you” — the person already knows you mean them:  “Go to the market.”
You can add details to make it clear:  “Exit the court quietly.”
To be polite, add please:  “Please wash your hands.”
To say *not* to do something, use don't:  “Don't run in the hall.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You go to the hospital.”  ✗
Fix: Leave out “you” for a command: “Go to the hospital.”  ✓
Mistake: “Quiet in the court.”  ✗   Fix: Don't forget the verb: “Be quiet in the court.”  ✓
Memory tip
Imperatives are “action words for clear instructions.” If you can picture someone doing the action, you are using the imperative correctly!
Practice 1
Choose the correct verb to complete each instruction:
______ your hands before eating.  (Wash / Washing)
______ the door when you leave.  (Close / Closes)
______ to the teacher if you have a question.  (Ask / Asking)
______ your phone during class.  (Use / Don't use)
Lesson 2:  Simple Present Tense
What it means and why it matters

We use the simple present tense to talk about things that happen again and again, or facts that are always true. It helps us describe routines and how our community works, like “The market opens at 7 a.m.”
How to form it
Use the base verb with: I, you, we, they.
They play football.
We visit the market.
I live in a village.
Add -s (or -es) with: he, she, it.
The doctor works at the hospital.
She visits her grandmother.
The market opens early.
If a verb ends in -y after a consonant, change y to i and add -es: study → studies.
A common mistake
Mistake: “The doctor work at the hospital.”  ✗
Fix: he/she/it needs the extra “s”: “The doctor works at the hospital.”  ✓
Memory tip
This is your “everyday tense.” If you do it every day, use the simple present. And remember: “s” for he, she, it!
Practice 2
Write the correct form of the verb in brackets:
My brother ______ (play) football after school.
She ______ (study) maths every day.
The sun ______ (rise) in the east.
They ______ (go) to the market on Fridays.
The nurse ______ (help) sick people.
Lesson 3:  Present Simple Questions
What it means and why it matters
Good learners ask questions! Asking questions helps us find information and understand our community — like “Where is the college?” or “Do you know the address?”
How to form it
Use do with I, you, we, they:  “Do you visit the market?”
Use does with he, she, it:  “Does she work at the hospital?”
After do/does, use the base verb (no “s”):  “Does he play football?”
You can start with a question word — what, where, when, who, how:  “Where does the doctor work?”
With the verb *to be*, just move is/are to the front:  “Is the hospital open?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Does you like the market?”  ✗   Fix: use do for “you”: “Do you like the market?”  ✓
Mistake: “Where he is?”  ✗   Fix: “Where is he?”  ✓
Memory tip
“Do” is the friendly helper for many people (I, you, we, they). “Does” is for the one person who needs extra help (he, she, it).
Practice 3
Complete each question with do or does and the base verb in brackets:
______ you ______ (like) the market?
______ he ______ (play) football?
______ they ______ (visit) the college?
______ she ______ (know) the address?
Lesson 4:  Using “can see” + object
What it means and why it matters
We use “can see” to say what we are able to look at and notice around us. It makes our descriptions come alive: “I can see the border,” “She can see the garden from the market.”
How to form it
Subject + can + see + object (the thing you see).
I can see the market.
We can see the mountains.
She can see the garden from the window.
To ask: “Can you see the border?”   To say no: “I cannot see the border.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She cans see the garden.”  ✗   Fix: “can” never adds an “s”: “She can see the garden.”  ✓
Mistake: “I can see.” (with nothing after it)  ✗   Fix: add the object: “I can see the border.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “can see” as your “magic eyes.” Always tell people *what* your magic eyes spot: Subject + can + see + what you see.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with can see and finish the idea:
From the bus, I ______ the market.
They ______ the animals at the farm.
We ______ the garden from here.
She ______ the border on the map.
Lesson 5:  Using “because” for Explanations
What it means and why it matters
The little word “because” is very powerful. It explains *why* something happens or why we feel a certain way. For example: “I like the officer because he helps people.”
How to form it
Say your idea:  “I like the officer.”
Add because:  “I like the officer because …”
Give the reason:  “… he helps people.”
Full sentence:  “I like the officer because he helps people.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I like the market. It has fruit.” (two short sentences, no reason word)
Fix: join them with because: “I like the market because it has fruit.”  ✓
Also, “because” cannot stand alone: “Because it rained.” ✗ is not a full sentence. Say “We stayed home because it rained.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “because” as a bridge. It connects your idea to the reason behind it. Every time you want to explain *why*, cross the “because” bridge.
Practice 5
Finish each sentence with your own reason using “because”:
I like my teacher ______.
The market is my favourite place ______.
I want to be a ______ ______.
Lesson 6:  Descriptive Adjectives and Opposites
What it means and why it matters
Descriptive adjectives are words that describe nouns (people, places, things). Instead of “the village,” we can say “the quiet village.” Opposites help us compare two places clearly.
How to form it
Pick a noun to describe:  “village.”
Choose an adjective:  “quiet.”
Find its opposite:  the opposite of “quiet” is “busy.”
Show the contrast in one sentence:  “The village is quiet, but the county center is busy.”
Useful opposites for this unit: quiet ↔ busy, small ↔ big, public ↔ private.
A common mistake
Mistake: “The village is more quiet than the town.”  ✗
Fix: short words add -er: “The village is quieter than the town.”  ✓
Memory tip
Picture the two places in your mind: a “quiet village” with soft sounds, and a “busy market” full of people. Seeing the difference helps you remember the words.
Practice 6
Fill each blank with quiet or busy:
The garden is ______; the market is ______.
My room is ______; the county center is ______.
Lesson 7:  Quantifiers (some, a lot of)
What it means and why it matters
Quantifiers tell us *how much* or *how many*. They help us say whether there is a small amount or a large amount of something in our community.
How to form it
Use some for an amount that is not exact:  “Some counties have many villages.”
Use a lot of for a large amount:  “A lot of people visit the market.”
Both can be used with things you can count (people, villages) and things you cannot count (water).
A common mistake
Mistake: “I do not have some friends.”  ✗
Fix: in a negative sentence, use any: “I do not have any friends.”  ✓  (Use some in positive sentences and questions.)
Memory tip
Think of some as a little sprinkle, and a lot of as a big pile.
Practice 7
Fill each blank with some or a lot of:
There are ______ children playing in the garden.  (a large crowd)
I need ______ help with my homework.  (a little)
The market has ______ fruit today.  (a huge amount)
We saw ______ birds near the border.  (just a few)
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Imperatives)
1. Wash   2. Close   3. Ask   4. Don't use
Practice 2 (Simple Present)
1. plays   2. studies   3. rises   4. go   5. helps
Practice 3 (Present Simple Questions)
Do you like the market?
Does he play football?

paragraphs: Unit 4: Places and Community
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will explore the places in your community and the people who help you there. You will learn to name places like the hospital, the market, the court, the college and the garden, and to talk about helpers like the doctor, the officer and the sailor. You will describe places using words like quiet and busy, give clear instructions, ask questions, and explain your ideas with the word “because”. By the end, you will be ready to write your own report about a place in your community — just like Tatu does in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name places in your community and the people who work there.
Describe a place using adjectives and opposites, like quiet and busy.
Give simple instructions using imperatives (“Exit the court quietly.”).
Talk about routines using the simple present (“The doctor works at the hospital.”).
Ask questions with do and does (“Where does the college open?”).
Say what you can see, using can see (“I can see the market.”).
Explain your reasons using because.
Use some and a lot of to talk about amounts.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few community words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “From Our Village to the County”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — Our Community
Read this short text:

A community is a place where people live and help one another. In my county there are many places. At the hospital, a doctor helps people who are sick. At the market, people buy and sell food. At the college, older students learn new jobs. An officer keeps us safe on the road. We all live together, and we all help one another.
After reading, answer:
Name three places from the text. What happens at each one?
Who keeps people safe on the road?
What places are there in your own community?
Reading 2 — A Poem: “Places I Know”
In my village the days are slow,
Past the garden the goats all go.
The market is busy, the court is grand,
So many places across our land!
After reading, answer:
Which places does the poem name?
Is the village slow or busy in the poem? Is the market slow or busy?
What place near your home would you put in a poem? (You will write your own poem later.)
Listening 1 — At the Market
Listen to Aisha talk about her trip. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“On Friday we go to the market. It is busy! We buy bananas and rice. I can see a lot of people. I like the market because it is full of colours.”
What does Aisha buy at the market?
Is the market quiet or busy? Why does Aisha like it?
Listening 2 — Community Helpers Song
Sing along:
“The doctor helps you when you're sick, the officer keeps you safe. The sailor sails across the sea — our helpers make one place!”
Which three helpers are in the song? What does each one do?
Who is another helper in your community that is not in the song?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — My Community
Get ready to say:
“In my community there is a ______.”
“At the ______, people ______.”
“I like the ______ because ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place clearly? Did you give a reason with “because”? Did you speak loudly enough?
Speaking 2 — Giving Directions
Use imperatives to give simple directions to a place. Say them out loud:
“Go straight to the market.”
“Turn left at the hospital.”
“Exit through the big door.”
Speaking Game — Guess the Place
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a community place and gives a clue: “A doctor works here.”
The other person guesses: “Is it the hospital?”
Answer: “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — Describe a Place
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
The ______ is a place where ______.
It is (quiet / busy).
I like it because ______.
At the ______ I can see ______.
Writing 2 — Your Own Poem
Write your own version of “Places I Know”. Change the places to ones near your home:
In my ______ the days are slow, / Past the ______ the ______ all go. / The ______ is busy, the ______ is grand, / So many places across our land!
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
Writing 3 — A Report
Like Tatu in the story, write a short report about a place you visited. Write three or four sentences with details. Begin: “Today I visited the ______.”
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Who Works Here? (Match)
Match each helper to the place they work. Write the pairs.
doctor  →  ______
officer  →  ______
sailor  →  ______
college student  →  ______
Places: ship · hospital · college · road (or court).
Answers: 1. hospital  2. road/court  3. ship  4. college
Activity 2 — True or False (Community Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A doctor helps people who are sick.
A market is a place where people learn to swim.
A village is bigger than a county.
An exit is a way to go out of a place.
Answers: 1. True  2. False (a place to buy and sell things)  3. False (a village is smaller)  4. True
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
Choose from: hospital · market · border · address · garden.
We buy fruit and vegetables at the ______.
The doctor works at the ______.
Flowers and tomatoes grow in the ______.
I wrote my home ______ on the letter.
The line where two counties meet is the ______.
Answers: 1. market  2. hospital  3. garden  4. address  5. border
Activity 4 — Quiet or Busy?
Write “quiet” or “busy” for each place. There is no single right answer — explain your choice with “because”. Example: “The village is quiet because there are few people.”
a village
a big market
a garden early in the morning
the county center at noon
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about community places?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name places in my community and who works there.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can describe a place using words like quiet and busy.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give an instruction using an imperative.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use the simple present (“the doctor works”).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask a question with “do” or “does”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what I can see, and explain a reason with “because”.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 5
Make sure you know all the Unit 4 words on your Word Wall.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
In Unit 5, “Actions and Activities”, you will talk about the things people do. Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 5? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟
