paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 4: Places and Community
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “From Our Village to the County”
From Our Village to the County
Tatu lived in a small village near Mount Meru in northern Tanzania. The village was quiet, with tall trees, green farms, and goats walking slowly along the road.

Every Friday, Tatu went with her class to learn about the community. One morning, their teacher said, “Today, we will visit the county center.” The whole class clapped. Tatu had never been there before.
They climbed into a big school bus and set off. “Look out of the window,” said the teacher. “What can you see?”
“I can see the market,” said Musa. “There are a lot of people!”
“I can see a garden,” said Asha. “It has flowers and trees.”
Their first stop was the hospital. A kind nurse met them at the door. “This is a public hospital,” she said. “Many people visit here every day.”
Tatu raised her hand. “Who works here?” she asked.
The nurse smiled. “The doctor works here. He helps people who are sick.” The class walked through the hall and passed clean rooms with beds, medicine, and smiling workers.
“Exit the hospital quietly,” said the teacher. “People are resting.”
Next, they walked to the court. “What happens here?” asked Asha.
“This is where problems are solved fairly,” said the teacher. “An officer and a judge work here.” The building was large and painted white. A sign read: “Court — County of Arusha.”
Inside, they sat quietly on the wooden benches. “Can you see the big chair?” asked the teacher. “That is where the judge sits.”
After the court, they walked to the market. Sellers called out beside baskets of bananas, maize, and rice. “This place is busy,” said Tatu. “Our village market is much smaller.”
“Some markets are small,” said the teacher, “and others have a lot of things.” They bought sweet mangoes and sat near a small garden to eat.
“Next is the college,” said the teacher.
“What is a college?” asked Musa.
“It is a school for older students,” said the teacher. “There, they learn to be nurses, builders, or officers.”
At the college gate, a friendly student greeted them. “My name is Juma,” he said. “I study health and safety.”
“Do you want to be a doctor?” asked Asha.
“Yes,” said Juma, “because I want to help my village.”
As they walked back to the bus, Tatu saw a sign by the road. “What is the border?” she asked.
“It is where one county ends and another begins,” said the teacher. “That is the border between Arusha and Kilimanjaro counties.”
When they returned to school, the teacher said, “Now write about what you saw.” Tatu picked up her pencil and wrote:

“Today we went from our village to the county center. We saw a hospital where the doctor works. We visited the court, the college, the market, and a garden. I learned that many people work to help us. I like the officer because he protects us. One day I want to be a sailor and see more places — but I will always come home.”
The teacher smiled at her paper. “This is a good report,” she said. “It has details, and it shows what you learned.”
That evening, Tatu shared her writing with her grandmother. “Very nice,” said Grandma. “Your address may say ‘village,’ but your ideas travel far.”
Tatu laughed. “Maybe one day I will see the sea, meet a real sailor, or even cross a border,” she said.
Her grandmother nodded gently. “Yes. But never forget your village roots.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Where does Tatu live? Is her village quiet or busy?
Which place did the class visit first? Who works there?
Why did the teacher say, “Exit the hospital quietly”?
What does an officer and a judge do at the court?
Why does Juma want to become a doctor? (Use “because” in your answer.)
What is a border? Which two counties meet at the border in the story?
Think and talk
Tatu said her village market is smaller than the county market. How is your community the same as, or different from, Tatu's village?
Grandma said, “Never forget your village roots.” What do you think she means?
Which community helper would you most like to be — a doctor, an officer, or a sailor? Why?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 4 words and say the sentence they are in:
village
county
market
garden
hospital
doctor
court
officer
college
border
sailor
address
exit
Now You Try:  Writing
Write a short report about a place in your community, just like Tatu did. Start with: “Today I visited the ______.” Write three or four sentences with details.
Draw one place from the story — the hospital, the court, the market, or the college. Write one sentence under your picture about who works there.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 5: Actions and Activities
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson at a time, try its practice, and then check your answers at the bottom of this file. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Take your time; you do not have to do them all in one day.
Lesson 1:  The Past Simple Tense
What it means and why it matters

We use the past simple to talk about actions that already happened and are finished. It helps us tell stories and share things we did. In this unit's story, the children built a wall — that already happened, so we say built, not build.
How to form it
For most verbs, add -ed:  discuss → discussed, listen → listened, celebrate → celebrated.
Some verbs are special (irregular). You must learn these:  build → built,  go → went,  have → had.
Often we add a time word:  yesterday, last week, a long time ago.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Yesterday they build a house.”  ✗
Fix: The action is finished, so use the past form: “Yesterday they built a house.”  ✓
Memory tip
The past simple is a little time machine. If it is done, take it back in time — add -ed for most verbs, and remember the special ones like built and went.
Practice 1
Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets:
Last summer they ______ (build) a reading wall.
She ______ (celebrate) her birthday with friends.
He ______ (remove) the old sign yesterday.
We ______ (listen) to the teacher this morning.
Lesson 2:  Sentence Starters for Explanations
What it means and why it matters
Sometimes we need to explain why something happened or when it happened. Special starter words like because, when, since and as help us do this. They make our sentences clearer and more interesting.
How to use it
because → gives the reason:  “I escaped because I was scared.”
when → tells the time:  “It happened when it was dark.”
since → gives a reason:  “She was happy since she got good news.”
as → time or reason:  “They laughed as they watched the show.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I left because.”  ✗  (The thought is not finished.)
Fix: Always finish the idea: “I left because I was tired.”  ✓
Memory tip
A starter word is a door — but you must always walk through it. After because, always say the rest of the reason.
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with because, when, since or as:
I ran fast ______ I wanted to catch the bus.
We stayed inside ______ it was too cold.
He was quiet ______ it was a serious moment.
It happened ______ it was dark outside.
Lesson 3:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Good learners ask questions! Wh- questions begin with what, where, who, when, why and how. They help us ask for real information, not just a yes or no answer.
How to form it
What happened?  (asking about a thing or event)
Where did you travel?  (asking about a place — note the helping word did)
Who offered help?  (asking about a person)
When did you finish?  ·  Why did the wall shake?  ·  How did you fix it?
A common mistake
Mistake: “Where you traveled?”  ✗  (missing the helping word)
Fix: In past questions, use did and the base verb: “Where did you travel?”  ✓
Memory tip
Wh- words want answers! The Wh- word always comes first, at the front of the question.
Practice 3
Complete each question with the correct Wh- word:
______ is your favourite colour?
______ did you go last weekend?
______ helped you with your project?
______ did the wall shake? (ask for a reason)
Lesson 4:  Cause and Effect (because / so)
What it means and why it matters
We often want to show why something happens (the cause) and what happens next (the effect). Because gives the reason. So shows the result.
How to use it
because → reason:  “He removed the trash because it smelled.”
so → result:  “She offered help, so I listened.”
Put a comma before so when it joins two full ideas.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She was happy so she smiled.”  ✗  (no comma)
Fix: “She was happy, so she smiled.”  ✓
Memory tip
Because explains the reason. So shows what happens. “Because explains, So shows!”
Practice 4
Fill in the blank with because or so:
The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones.
He was late ______ he missed the bus.
The flowers wilted ______ they did not get water.
I forgot my homework, ______ I felt sad.
Lesson 5:  Time Words for Sequencing
What it means and why it matters
Time words tell us the order of events — what came first, next and last. They help us tell a clear story, just like the children who planned and built the wall step by step.
How to use it
First, they searched for smooth stones.
Then, they discussed the design and colours.
Next, they began to build.
Finally, they celebrated together.
A common mistake
Mistake: “They started to build. First, they discussed the idea.”  ✗  (wrong order)
Fix: Put the events in the right order: “First, they discussed the idea. Then, they started to build.”  ✓
Memory tip
Time words are a little story map: First → Next → Then → Finally. Follow the map and no one gets lost.
Practice 5
Put the time words in order. Fill in with First, Next, Then or Finally:
______, they gathered their materials.
______, they discussed the plan.
______, they built the wall.
______, they celebrated their success!
Lesson 6:  Polite Opinions
What it means and why it matters
When we share what we think — especially if we disagree — it is kind to say it gently. Polite opinion phrases help us share ideas in a friendly, respectful way.
How to use it
I think …  →  “I think traveling is fun.”
I don't think …  →  “I don't think escaping was the best idea.”
In my opinion …  or  I believe …  →  “In my opinion, reading is important.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Traveling is boring!”  ✗  (sounds strong and unkind)
Fix: Soften it: “I think traveling can be boring sometimes.”  ✓
Memory tip
A polite opinion is like a soft pillow. Adding I think makes your idea comfortable for everyone to hear.
Practice 6
Rewrite each strong sentence as a polite opinion using I think or I don't think:
Group work is fun.
Staying up late is a good idea.
This story is exciting.
Lesson 7:  Word Families
What it means and why it matters
Word families are groups of words that grow from the same base word. If you learn one word, you can unlock several others! This makes your vocabulary much bigger.
How to use it
Add -er for the person who does it:  build → builder.
Add -ment for the result:  develop → development.
Add -tion for the process:  protect → protection;  celebrate → celebration.
A common mistake
Mistake: “I want to buildment a sandcastle.”  ✗
Fix: Use the base word for the action: “I want to build a sandcastle.”  ✓
Memory tip
A word family is a tree. The base word is the trunk, and each ending (-er, -ment, -tion) is a branch that grows its own meaning.
Practice 7
Write the correct form of the word in brackets:
The ______ (build) is making a new house.
The ______ (develop) of the garden took many weeks.
A helmet gives good ______ (protect).
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Past Simple)
1. built   2. celebrated   3. removed   4. listened
Practice 2 (Sentence Starters)
1. because   2. because   3. since   4. when   (Note: other polite answers can work — ask your AI tutor.)
Practice 3 (Wh- Questions)
1. What   2. Where   3. Who   4. Why
Practice 4 (because / so)
1. so   2. because   3. because   4. so
Practice 5 (Time Words)
1. First   2. Then   3. Next   4. Finally
Practice 6 (Polite Opinions) — example answers
I think group work is fun.
I don't think staying up late is a good idea.
I think this story is exciting.
Practice 7 (Word Families)
1. builder   2. development   3. protection

paragraphs: Unit 5: Actions and Activities
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 5! This unit is all about actions and activities — the things people DO. You will learn lots of action words (verbs) such as build, discuss, protect and celebrate. You will talk about things you did, using the past simple, and learn to explain why things happened using words like because and so. You will put events in order with First, Next, Then and Finally, ask Wh- questions, and share your ideas politely. By the end, you will be ready to read this unit's story, “The Wall Behind the Garden”, where a class of children work together to build something special.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and use many action words, such as build, listen, protect and celebrate.
Talk about things you did, using the past simple (built, listened, celebrated).
Explain why something happened, using because and so.
Put events in order, using First, Next, Then, Finally.
Ask questions with what, where, who, when, why and how.
Share your ideas politely, using I think and I don't think.
Read and understand a story about working together to solve a problem.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few action words each day.
Do the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one or two at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Wall Behind the Garden”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — A Busy Saturday
Read this short text:

On Saturday, our family had a busy day. First, we cleaned the yard together. My brother removed the old sticks, and I helped build a small stone path. Then we searched the garden for ripe tomatoes. Finally, we celebrated our hard work with sweet tea and laughing. It was a wonderful day!
After reading, answer:
What did the family do first? What did they do finally?
Find three action words in the text. (Hint: look for words like removed, build, searched.)
Which time words help you follow the order? (First … Then … Finally)
Reading 2 — Helping Hands
Read this short text:

Amina saw her neighbour carrying a heavy basket. “Can I offer some help?” she asked politely. Together they carried the basket home. The neighbour smiled. “Thank you! You have a kind heart.” Amina felt happy because she had helped someone.
After reading, answer:
What did Amina offer to do?
Why did Amina feel happy? (Use the word because in your answer.)
What kind thing could you offer to do for someone at home this week?
Listening 1 — Planning the Garden
Listen to two friends make a plan. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“First, we will search for good seeds. Then, we will build a small fence to protect the plants. After that, we will water the garden every day. Finally, we will celebrate when the first flower grows!”
How many steps are in the plan? Say them in order.
Why do the friends build a fence? (to protect the plants)
What will they do at the very end?
Listening 2 — What Happened?
Listen to Mosi tell his teacher about the wind. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud.)
“Yesterday the wind blew hard and the wall began to shake. We ran to hold the frame. We did not complete the support, so we will fix it today. I think we should add more stones.”
What happened to the wall yesterday?
Why will they fix it today? (Listen for the word so.)
What is Mosi's polite opinion? What words tell you it is polite? (I think)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Tell What You Did
Think of something you did yesterday or last week. Get ready to say three sentences in order:
“Finally, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use the past simple? Did you use time words? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Share a Polite Opinion
Choose a topic (a game, a food, a story). Say what you think, politely:
“I think ______ because ______.”
“I don't think ______.”
Speaking Game — What Happened Next?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person starts a story: “The goat tried to escape from the pen …”
The next person adds one action: “Then it ran to the garden and ______.”
Keep taking turns, using action words, until the story is finished. Ask “What happened next?” each time.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — How To Do Something
Write four steps to do a simple activity, such as how to plant a seed or how to tidy your room. Use time words:
Finally, ______.
Writing 2 — Something You Built or Made
Write three or four sentences about a time you built, made, or fixed something. Answer these questions in your writing:
What did you build or make?
Who helped you?
What happened while you were doing it?
How did you feel when you completed it?
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Action Word
Match each action word to what it means. Write the letter next to the number.
protect      (a) to look for something
search       (b) to finish something
complete   (c) to keep something safe
celebrate   (d) to do something happy for a special time
Answers: 1-c   2-a   3-b   4-d
Activity 2 — Past Simple
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday we ______ (build) a stone path.
She ______ (celebrate) her birthday last week.
The children ______ (listen) to the teacher.
He ______ (remove) the broken fence.
Answers: 1. built   2. celebrated   3. listened   4. removed
Activity 3 — because or so?
Fill in each blank with because or so.
The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones.
I wore a helmet ______ I wanted to protect my head.
It started to rain, ______ we went inside.
She smiled ______ she was happy.
Answers: 1. so   2. because   3. so   4. because
Activity 4 — Put the Steps in Order
These sentences about building the wall are mixed up. Write them in the correct order using First, Then, Next, Finally.
They celebrated together.
They searched for smooth stones.
They discussed the design.
They began to build.
Answer: First, they searched for smooth stones. Then, they discussed the design. Next, they began to build. Finally, they celebrated together.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to me tell what I did yesterday and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more sentences to practise the past simple?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use many action words in sentences.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about things I did, using the past simple.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain why, using because and so.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can put events in order, using First, Then, Finally.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask Wh- questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can share my ideas politely with “I think”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can read and understand the story.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 6
In Unit 6, “Describing People and Things”, you will learn describing words (adjectives) — words like tall, kind, sunny and brave — to describe people, places and things around you.
Make sure you know all the Unit 5 action words on your Word Wall.
Start noticing describing words in your story: strong wall, smooth stones, dark clouds.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 6? Can you get me ready to describe people and things?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 5: Actions and Activities
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Wall Behind the Garden”
The Wall Behind the Garden
It was the first week of school in a small village. The rain had stopped, and the fields were dry and warm. The children helped sweep the schoolyard and water the garden behind the classrooms.
That morning, the teacher smiled and said, “Today we will start a special project. Together, we will build a new reading wall in the garden.”
“What will be on the wall?” asked Halima.
“Stories, poems, and pictures,” said the teacher. “But first, we must remove the old, broken fence.”
The class went outside. Some children pulled weeds. Others removed old wires and sticks. Then they saw something strange — a hole in the ground, covered by stones and dry leaves.
“What happened here?” asked Mosi, looking closely.
“I don't know,” said Halima. “Let's discuss it with the teacher.”
The teacher looked at the hole and said, “A long time ago, this was an animal path. Goats used to escape from this yard right here.”
The children laughed. “We don't want goats in our classroom!”
“Then we must block the path well,” said the teacher. “Let's plan carefully and build a strong wall.”
First, they searched for smooth, flat stones. Then, they discussed the design and the colours. Finally, they began to build.
Halima painted birds. Mosi drew a blue waterfall. Some children wrote short poems.
But suddenly the clouds turned dark. The wind blew hard, and the wall began to shake.
“Hold the frame!” shouted the teacher. Halima ran to help. Together they held the pieces until the wind slowed down.
“We need to fix it,” said Mosi. “We didn't complete the support.”
“I think we should add more stones,” said Halima.
“I don't think paint alone is strong enough to hold it,” said another child.
The next day, everyone came early. “We will finish today,” said the teacher. “Let's be strong and work together.” They completed the wall and made it much stronger.
The final touch was a sign that read: “We built this wall to share words and protect dreams.”
Later that week, the whole village came to celebrate. There was singing, clapping, and food from every family.
“This is a true celebration,” said the teacher. “You all helped. You worked hard.”
“Halima's drawing is my favourite,” said the principal.
“And Mosi's poem,” said the teacher. “He wrote about how we protect our stories and our voices.”
After the event, Halima saw a younger girl standing near the wall.
“Can I draw here one day?” the girl asked shyly.
“Of course,” said Halima kindly. “This wall is for everyone.”

That night, Halima wrote in her notebook: “We built something beautiful. It was windy and messy. But we listened to each other. We shared our ideas. We offered help. And we finished something strong.”
The next morning, a visitor came to the school. He had travelled from a nearby town.
“I work in education,” he said. “I heard you made something special.” The students proudly gave him a tour of the wall.
“You are helping this place develop,” he said. “This wall is not just a wall. It is a gift.”
When he left, Mosi asked, “Why did he call it a gift?”
“Because it gives people ideas,” said the teacher. “And new ideas are part of how a village grows and develops.”
Halima nodded and smiled. “And we made it,” she said. “Together.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What special project did the class start in the garden?
What did the children have to remove first, before they could build?
What strange thing did they find in the ground? What had escaped there long ago?
What went wrong while they were building? How did they fix it?
What did the sign on the wall say?
Why did the visitor call the wall “a gift”?
Think and talk
The children said, “We listened to each other. We offered help.” Why did this help them finish the wall?
Halima told the younger girl, “This wall is for everyone.” Why was that a kind thing to say?
Tell about a time you worked together with others to finish something. What did each person do?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 5 words and say the sentence they are in:
build
remove
happen
discuss
escape
search
complete
protect
celebrate
listen
offer
develop
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about a time you built or made something. Use the past simple (for example: “First, I ______. Then, I ______.”).
Imagine you could add one thing to the reading wall — a poem, a picture, or a story. Write two sentences about what you would add and why.
Draw the finished wall with Halima's birds and Mosi's waterfall. Write one sentence under your picture.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 6: Describing People and Things
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. They all help you describe people and things. Read one lesson, say the example sentences out loud, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Do one or two lessons a day — you do not have to do them all at once!
Lesson 1:  The Past Simple Tense
What it means and why it matters
We use the past simple to talk about things that already happened. It helps us tell stories and describe what people did — like Neema, who helped the boy who fell.
How to form it
Most verbs just add -ed:  play → played,  help → helped,  walk → walked.
Some verbs are special (irregular) and change all over:  go → went,  have → had,  see → saw.
The verb to be changes too:  is / am → was,  are → were.
Examples: “She was kind to her classmate.”  ·  “They played outside yesterday.”  ·  “He went to the store.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He goed to the store.”  ✗
Fix: go is a special verb: “He went to the store.”  ✓
Memory tip
The past simple is a time machine. When you use it, you travel back to something that already happened.
Practice 1
Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets:
Last week, I ______ (visit) my grandmother.
She ______ (finish) her homework early.
They ______ (play) football at the park.
He ______ (go) to the shop yesterday.
Lesson 2:  The Word ‘Could’
What it means and why it matters
Could is a helper word. We use it to talk about things that were possible in the past, or to ask for something in a polite way.
How to form it
Past ability:  “When I was five, I could swim.”
Something possible:  “They could be calm even on rough days.”
Polite request:  “Could you lend me your pencil, please?”
Always use the plain verb after could:  could play,  could be,  could help.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He could plays football.”  ✗
Fix: use the plain verb: “He could play football.”  ✓
Memory tip
Could is like a time traveller — it talks about what was possible before, or what might happen.
Practice 2
Fill in the blank using could + the verb in brackets:
When I was little, I ______ (ride) my bike without help.
If we practise every day, we ______ (be) champions!
______ you please help me with my project?
Lesson 3:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions start with who, what, when, where, why or how. They help us ask for exactly the information we want. Good learners ask lots of questions!
How to form it
The order is:  Wh- word + verb + subject.
Who → asks about a person:  “Who is your best friend?”
What → asks about a thing:  “What makes someone honest?”
Why → asks for a reason:  “Why is he so friendly?”
How → asks about the way:  “How do you get to school?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Why he is friendly?”  ✗
Fix: put the verb before the subject: “Why is he friendly?”  ✓
Memory tip
The Wh- words are your question detectives: who, what, when, where, why, how.
Practice 3
Choose the correct Wh- word:
______ is your favourite colour? (What / Where)
______ do you go to bed? (When / How)
______ is your best friend? (Who / Why)
______ do you like the story? (How / Why)
Lesson 4:  Comparing Two Things
What it means and why it matters
A comparative compares two things. It lets us say one thing is bigger, kinder or rougher than another.
How to form it
Short words add -er:  tall → taller,  rough → rougher.
Words ending in -y change y to i, then add -er:  happy → happier,  funny → funnier.
Long words use more:  helpful → more helpful,  careful → more careful.
Then use than:  “My dog is bigger than my cat.”
A few words are special:  good → better,  bad → worse.
A common mistake
Mistake: “My dog is more big than my cat.”  ✗
Fix: short words add -er: “My dog is bigger than my cat.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of -er as a little “comparison badge” you clip onto short adjectives. For long words, add more — like adding “extra” to your comparison.
Practice 4
Write the comparative form of the word in brackets:
This puzzle is ______ (easy) than that one.
She is ______ (smart) than her brother.
This story is ______ (funny) than the last one.
Lesson 5:  Present Simple Statements
What it means and why it matters
We use the present simple to describe people and things, and to talk about what is true now. It is perfect for describing a friend: “My friend is kind.”
How to form it
Use is for one person or thing:  “My friend is kind and careful.”
Use are for more than one:  “Popular kids are not always kind.”
With he, she, it, action verbs add -s:  “She plays the piano.”  ·  “He helps everyone.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He play football.”  ✗
Fix: add -s for he/she/it: “He plays football.”  ✓
Memory tip
S for singular! For he, she or it, remember to add -s to the action verb.
Practice 5
Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb in brackets:
My sister ______ (like) to read books.
The dogs ______ (be) very friendly.
My parents ______ (enjoy) cooking together.
He ______ (help) his friends every day.
Lesson 6:  Telling What Someone Said
What it means and why it matters
Reported speech is how we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words. It is useful for telling stories: “She said her cousin was busy.”
How to form it
Start with a reporting word:  said, told, asked.
Change the verb back one step in time:  “I am happy” → “She said she was happy.”
Change the pronoun to match:  “My cousin is busy” → “She said her cousin was busy.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She said her cousin is busy.”  ✗
Fix: change the verb to the past: “She said her cousin was busy.”  ✓
Memory tip
Say it like a rhyme: “Change the tense, change the pronoun!”
Practice 6
Fill in the blank to finish the reported sentence:
Direct: “I love ice cream.”  →  Reported: He said he ______ ice cream.
Direct: “Where are you going?”  →  Reported: She asked where I ______ going.
Direct: “Don’t touch that!”  →  Reported: He told me ______ touch that.
Lesson 7:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
What it means and why it matters
An imperative is a command or instruction. Your teacher uses them all the time: “Describe your favourite classmate.” They tell someone exactly what to do.
How to form it
Start with the plain verb — no subject:  “Describe your best friend.”
Use clear action words:  List, Write, Draw, Explain.
To be polite, add please:  “Please describe your favourite classmate.”
To say “don’t”, use Do not / Don’t:  “Don’t forget to add adjectives.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You describe your favourite classmate.”  ✗
Fix: leave out the subject: “Describe your favourite classmate.”  ✓
Memory tip
An imperative is like pointing at a friend and saying the action straight out: “Describe!”
Practice 7
Fill in the blank with a verb from the box:  (Describe · List · Draw · Write)
______ your favourite animal.
______ three things you love about school.
______ a picture of your family.
______ words that describe a good friend.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Past Simple)
1. visited   2. finished   3. played   4. went
Practice 2 (Could)
1. could ride   2. could be   3. Could
Practice 3 (Wh- Questions)
1. What   2. When   3. Who   4. Why
Practice 4 (Comparatives)
1. easier   2. smarter   3. funnier
Practice 5 (Present Simple)
1. likes   2. are   3. enjoy   4. helps
Practice 6 (Reported Speech)
1. loved   2. was   3. not to touch
Practice 7 (Imperatives)
1. Describe   2. List   3. Draw   4. Write

paragraphs: Unit 6: Describing People and Things
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will learn how to describe people and things. You will learn words for what a person is like on the inside — such as kind, honest and friendly — and words for what things are like on the outside — such as rough, tough and smooth. You will describe your favourite people, compare two things, and tell true stories about the past. By the end, you will be ready to enjoy this unit's story about Halima, a new girl who learns that carrying kindness makes everyone feel welcome.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use describing words (adjectives) to talk about people and things.
Describe your favourite person and say what makes them special.
Compare two people or things, using words like kinder and rougher than.
Talk about the past, using was, were and verbs like helped and went.
Use the word could to talk about what someone was able to do.
Ask Wh- questions (who, what, why, how) about people.
Write a short description of a person, using at least three adjectives.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few describing words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one or two a day.
Read the Story file, “The Girl Who Carried Kindness”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — My Cousin Yusuf
Read this short text:

My cousin Yusuf is my favourite person. He is kind and honest. He is always busy, but he still has time to help me. When I feel worried, he stays calm and speaks in a friendly voice. Yusuf is popular at school because he cares about everyone.
After reading, answer:
Who is the writer's favourite person?
Find three adjectives the writer uses to describe Yusuf.
Why is Yusuf popular at school?
Who is a kind person in your family? What are they like?
Reading 2 — Two Roads
Read this short text:

There are two roads near my home. The first road is smooth and easy to walk on. The second road is rough, with many bumps and stones. My father says his old boots are tough, so the rough road is not a problem for him. The two roads are not similar at all!
After reading, answer:
What is the first road like? What is the second road like?
Why is the rough road not a problem for the father?
Are the two roads similar or different?
Find something rough and something smooth near your own home.
Listening 1 — Describing a Friend
Listen to Amina describe her friend. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“My best friend is Hodan. She is very kind and friendly. Yesterday, I was sad, but Hodan was calm and she helped me. She is honest too — she always tells the truth. Hodan is my favourite friend!”
What is the name of Amina's best friend?
How did Hodan help Amina yesterday?
Amina says “Hodan was calm” — is she talking about now or about the past? (See Grammar Lesson 1.)
Listening 2 — Who Is Kinder?
Listen to two children compare their brothers.
“My brother is kind,” said Sagal. “But my brother is kinder!” said Liban. “He is friendlier than anyone in our street, and he could carry ten bags of rice when he was only twelve!”
Which two words compare people in this text? (Look for words ending in -er.)
What could Liban's brother do when he was twelve? (See Grammar Lesson 2 about could.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe Your Favourite Person
Get ready to say three or four sentences. You can start like this:
“My favourite person is ______.”
“He / She is ______ and ______.” (use two adjectives)
“He / She helps me when ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use at least two adjectives? Did you say who the person is and why you like them? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Describe a Thing
Pick up an object near you. Describe it in two or three sentences:
“This is my ______.”
“It is ______.” (for example: rough, smooth, tough, small)
“It is similar to a ______.”
Speaking Game — Guess Who
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of a person everyone knows, but keep it a secret.
Describe them with three adjectives: “This person is kind, busy and honest.”
The other person asks Wh- questions: “Is it a girl or a boy? Why do you like them?”
Keep going until they guess. Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — A Description of a Person
Write four sentences about a person you like. Use at least three adjectives from this unit. You can begin:
My favourite person is ______.
He / She is good at ______.
I like this person because ______.
Writing 2 — Comparing Two People
Write two sentences that compare two people you know. Use comparatives (see Grammar Lesson 4):
“______ is kinder than ______.”
“______ is busier than ______.”
Writing 3 — Sort the Traits

Make two lists. In the first list, write describing words about how someone acts or feels (for example: kind, honest, calm). In the second list, write describing words about how a thing looks or feels (for example: rough, tough, smooth). Check your lists with your AI tutor.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Choose the Right Word
Choose the best describing word for each sentence.
The rock was hard and ______ to break. (tough / calm)
She always tells the truth; she is very ______. (honest / rough)
He has many things to do today; he is ______. (busy / alive)
My shirt looks almost the same as yours; they are ______. (similar / careless)
Answers: 1. tough  2. honest  3. busy  4. similar
Activity 2 — Opposites
Match each word to its opposite.
careful  →  ______  (careless / kind)
rough  →  ______  (smooth / busy)
dead  →  ______  (alive / tough)
unkind  →  ______  (kind / rough)
Answers: 1. careless  2. smooth  3. alive  4. kind
Activity 3 — Past or Now?
Fill in the blank with is (now) or was (in the past).
Right now, my sister ______ busy with her homework.
Yesterday, Neema ______ kind to the new boy.
Today the road ______ rough after the rain.
Last week, the teacher ______ happy with our writing.
Answers: 1. is  2. was  3. is  4. was
Activity 4 — Describe and Draw
Draw a person from your family. Around your drawing, write four adjectives that describe them. Then read your words to your AI tutor or your teacher.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my description and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more sentences about comparing two things?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use describing words to talk about people.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can describe my favourite person and say why I like them.   Yes □   Not yet □

I can ask Wh- questions about a person.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a short description of a person.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 7
Next you will study Unit 7: Nature and the Environment. You will describe animals, plants and the weather — and the describing words you learned in this unit will help you a lot!
Make sure you know all the Unit 6 words on your Word Wall.
Look outside and try to describe one plant or animal with two adjectives.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 7? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — you are becoming a great describer! Every new word helps you say exactly what you mean. Keep practising, and be proud of how far you have come. 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 6: Describing People and Things
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Girl Who Carried Kindness”
The Girl Who Carried Kindness
The school bell rang, and the playground was full of noise. Children laughed, ran, shouted and played football. But in one corner, Halima sat alone. She held her lunchbox quietly.
She was new. Her family had just moved to the town, and nobody knew her yet.
That week, the teacher gave the class a writing task. “Describe your favourite person,” she said.
“Can it be someone in school?” asked David.
“Yes,” said the teacher. “Write with care. Be honest. Use adjectives.”
Halima thought for a moment. She did not have a favourite person yet.
The next day, something happened. Halima saw a girl pick up a fallen bag and give it back to its owner.
“She could have ignored it,” said another pupil.
“But she didn’t,” Halima whispered. “She cared.”
The girl’s name was Neema. She was one of the most popular pupils — clever, kind, and always busy helping others.
One afternoon, the class went out to the garden. A rough wind blew, and papers flew everywhere.
“Someone catch them!” shouted the teacher.
Most pupils ran after the papers, laughing. But Halima saw one boy trip over a chair. He looked hurt.
Neema did not laugh. She stopped and helped him sit up. “You’ll be okay,” she said in a calm, friendly voice.
Halima watched. Neema was strong and tough, but also gentle.
That night, Halima wrote in her notebook:
“She was kind to the boy who fell. She was friendly to the girl who dropped her bag. She could have laughed, but instead she showed care.”
The next day, Halima found the courage to speak to Neema. “Hi,” she said shyly. “I saw you help others. That was nice.”
Neema smiled. “Thank you. My mother says, ‘Kindness is free, so give extra when you can.’”
Halima smiled too.
The teacher gave a new task: “Compare two people in the class. Who is more helpful? Who is rougher?”
Some pupils wrote: “David is rougher than Neema.” Others wrote: “Neema is always calm and helpful.”
At the end of the week, the class shared their favourite-person descriptions. David said, “My favourite person is my uncle. He is tough, but he helps everyone.”
Then Halima stood up. “My favourite person is someone who carries kindness every day. She doesn’t show off. She just helps.”
“Who is it?” the teacher asked.
Halima pointed to Neema. “She gave back a bag. She helped a hurt boy. And she gave me a smile when I was new.”
Neema looked surprised. “No one told me that,” she said.
The teacher smiled. “Sometimes we forget the extra good we do.”
Later that day, Neema and Halima sat together under the tree. “You know,” said Neema, “you are kind too. You noticed what others didn’t.”
Halima laughed. “I used to feel alone. Now I feel a little more alive.”
The teacher told the class, “Describe someone again — but this time, include yourself. You matter too.”
Halima wrote:
“I am similar to Neema. I want to care. I want to grow stronger, not careless. I want to give kindness every day. Because I can.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Why was Halima sitting alone at the start of the story?
What writing task did the teacher give the class?
What kind thing did Neema do for the girl who dropped her bag, and for the boy who fell?
What does Neema’s mother say about kindness?
Who did Halima choose as her favourite person, and why?
How did Halima feel at the start, and how did she feel at the end? What changed?
Think and talk
The teacher said, “Sometimes we forget the extra good we do.” What good things do you do that you might forget?
Neema was tough but also gentle. How can a person be both strong and kind at the same time?
Halima wrote, “I want to give kindness every day.” What is one small act of kindness you could do tomorrow?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 6 words and read out the sentence they are in:
favourite
care
honest
popular
kind
busy
rough
calm
friendly
tough
extra
kindness
alive
similar
careless
Now You Try:  Writing
Describe your own favourite person. Write three sentences. Use at least three adjectives from this unit (for example: kind, honest, friendly).
Compare two people you know. Write one sentence using a comparative, like: “______ is kinder than ______.”
Draw Halima and Neema under the tree. Write one sentence under your picture about what makes each girl special.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 7: Nature and the Environment
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Nine grammar mini-lessons

This unit has nine small grammar lessons. Do not try to learn them all in one day! Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it. Everything here connects to nature and the environment.
Lesson 1:  The Present Perfect Tense
What it means and why it matters

We use the present perfect to talk about something that happened in the past but still matters now. When you say “I have explored a forest,” it shows this is part of who you are today. It helps you share your experiences with others.
How to form it
Formula:  subject + have / has + past participle
Use have with I, you, we, they:  “I have explored the coast.”
Use has with he, she, it:  “She has seen the mountain.”
For most verbs, the past participle ends in -ed (explore → explored). Some are irregular (see → seen).
A common mistake
Mistake: “I have see a bird.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle, not the base verb: “I have seen a bird.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the present perfect as a bridge from the past to today: have / has + the thing you did.
Practice 1
Write the present perfect. Use have or has + the correct past participle.
I ____ (explore) a cave.
They ____ (see) a shooting star.
She ____ (finish) her nature painting.
We ____ (visit) the forest.
Lesson 2:  Yes/No Questions with the Present Perfect
What it means and why it matters
These questions let us ask about someone's experiences without saying exactly when they happened. They are a great way to start a friendly conversation about nature.
How to form it
Formula:  Have / Has + subject + past participle + (extra words)?
“Have you ever visited the beach?”
“Has she seen the sunshine in winter?”
The word ever means “at any time in your life”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Has you ever been to the coast?”  ✗
Fix: use have with “you”: “Have you ever been to the coast?”  ✓
Memory tip
Have for I, you, we, they. Has for he, she, it. The helping verb jumps to the front to make a question.
Practice 2
Fill in have or has, and the past participle in brackets.
______ you ever ____ (go) to a mountain?
______ she ____ (see) that forest before?
______ they ____ (explore) the beach yet?
Lesson 3:  Short Answers
What it means and why it matters
Short answers are quick, simple replies. Instead of repeating the whole question, we answer in a few words. This makes talking smooth and natural.
How to form it
Use the same helping verb from the question, plus the subject.
“Have you explored a forest?”  →  “Yes, I have.” / “No, I haven't.”
“Has she seen the mountain?”  →  “Yes, she has.” / “No, she hasn't.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Yes, I have explored a forest.” (too long) or “Yes, I.” (no helping verb)  ✗
Fix: just say the helping verb: “Yes, I have.”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember the “A” rule: Always include the Auxiliary (helping) verb — have or has.
Practice 3
Write a short answer for each question. (Choose Yes or No.)
“Have you seen the sea?”  →  ______, I ______.
“Has your friend explored a cave?”  →  ______, he ______.
“Have they climbed the mountain?”  →  ______, they ______.
Lesson 4:  Irregular Past Participles
What it means and why it matters
Most verbs add -ed for the past participle, but some special verbs do not. These are irregular. We use them a lot with have and has, so it helps to learn them by heart.
How to form it
There is no single rule — you memorise them:
see → seen
be → been
freeze → frozen
go → gone    ·    eat → eaten    ·    write → written
A common mistake
Mistake: “I have saw that lake.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle: “I have seen that lake.”  ✓
Memory tip
Make flashcards: the base verb on one side, the past participle on the other. Test yourself every day.
Practice 4
Fill in with: seen, been, explored, frozen.
She has ____ to the mountains with her family.
The river has ____ over because of the cold.
I have ____ that big forest before.
They have ____ many caves on their trip.
Lesson 5:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are sentences that tell someone to do something. They are commands or requests, like “Protect the planet.” They help us give clear instructions.
How to form it
Start with the base verb (no “to”):  “Explore nature carefully.”
No subject needed — “you” is understood:  “Wear a hat.”
To say “do not”, start with Don't:  “Don't drop litter.”
Add please to be polite, or Let's to suggest doing it together.
A common mistake
Mistake: “You to close the door.” or “To explore the forest.”  ✗
Fix: just the base verb: “Close the door.” / “Explore the forest.”  ✓
Memory tip
An imperative is like a coach shouting instructions: verb first, straight to the point!
Practice 5
Write the imperative using the verb in brackets.
____ (protect) the planet.
____ (not drop) litter in the forest.
____ (listen) to your teacher.
____ (take) your water bottle on the trip.
Lesson 6:  Descriptive Phrases
What it means and why it matters
Descriptive phrases add detail so the reader can picture things clearly. Instead of just “rock”, we can say “a metal rock”. This makes your writing more interesting.
How to form it
Pick a noun:  rock.
Add an adjective that describes it:  metal.
Put them together:  “a metal rock on the beach.”
You can use two adjectives with a comma:  “a large, shiny rock.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “a small gray cat” (two adjectives, no comma) or no adjective at all.  ✗
Fix: add a describing word, and use a comma between two: “a small, gray cat.”  ✓
Memory tip
Descriptive words “add colour” to your sentence — the more you add, the brighter the picture.
Practice 6
Add a describing word to make each phrase more interesting.
a ____ mountain
the ____ weather
a ____ beach
Now rewrite this sentence with a descriptive phrase: “The bird sings.”
Lesson 7:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions start with who, what, where, when, why, or how. They help us ask for exact information and learn about the world.
How to form it
Choose the right wh- word: who (people), what (things), where (places), when (time), why (reasons), how (way).
Add a verb (is, are, do…):  “Where is the coast?”
Finish the question:  “What is the weather like today?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Where the coast?” (no verb)  ✗
Fix: add the verb: “Where is the coast?”  ✓
Memory tip
Say the rhyme: “Who, What, Where, When, Why, and How — ask them well, and you'll learn how!”
Practice 7
Fill in the correct wh- word.
____ is the tallest mountain you know?
____ is the weather like today?
____ do you go to enjoy nature?
____ is Earth special? (give a reason)
Lesson 8:  Quantifiers (How Much / How Many)
What it means and why it matters
Quantifiers tell us the amount of something. They make your sentences clear, so people know how much or how many you mean.
How to form it
For things you can count (mountains, trees), use many, a few, several:  “Some mountains are very high.”
For things you cannot count (sunshine, water), use much, a little, a lot of:  “A lot of sunshine is good for plants.”
Some and a lot of can be used for both.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Much children play outside.”  ✗
Fix: children can be counted, so use many: “Many children play outside.”  ✓
Memory tip
Count it? Use many. Can't count it? Use much. “A lot of” is a friendly word for both.
Practice 8
Choose the correct quantifier.
There are ____ (many / much) trees in the forest.
We need ____ (some / much) water for the trip.
She saw ____ (a few / a little) birds on the coast.
There is ____ (a little / many) sand on my shoes.
Lesson 9:  Reported Instructions
What it means and why it matters
Reported instructions tell someone what another person asked us to do. If the teacher says “Bring water,” you can tell your friend: “The teacher said to bring water.”
How to form it
Use said to or told (someone) to + the base verb:  “She told us to wear warm clothes.”
For a “don't”, use not to:  “He said not to run.”
Change the words for “me”, “us”, “my” so the sentence still makes sense.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He said explore the forest.” (no “to”)  ✗
Fix: add “to”: “He said to explore the forest.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “said to” as a bridge that carries the instruction from one person to another. Never forget the “to”.
Practice 9
Rewrite as a reported instruction.
“The teacher said, ‘Read the book.’”  →  The teacher said ____ the book.
“Dad told me, ‘Don't forget your hat!’”  →  Dad told me ____ my hat.
“She said, ‘Protect the planet.’”  →  She said ____ the planet.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Perfect)
1. have explored   2. have seen   3. has finished   4. have visited
Practice 2 (Yes/No Questions)
1. Have … gone   2. Has … seen   3. Have … explored
Practice 3 (Short Answers) — example answers
1. Yes, I have. / No, I haven't.   2. Yes, he has. / No, he hasn't.   3. Yes, they have. / No, they haven't.
Practice 4 (Irregular Past Participles)
1. been   2. frozen   3. seen   4. explored
Practice 5 (Imperatives)
1. Protect the planet.   2. Don't drop litter in the forest.   3. Listen to your teacher.   4. Take your water bottle on the trip.
Practice 6 (Descriptive Phrases) — example answers
1. a tall / snowy mountain   2. the sunny / cold weather   3. a sandy / rocky beach   4. e.g. “The small brown bird sings sweetly.”
Practice 7 (Wh- Questions)
1. What   2. What   3. Where   4. Why
Practice 8 (Quantifiers)
1. many   2. some   3. a few   4. a little
Practice 9 (Reported Instructions)
1. to read   2. not to forget   3. to protect

paragraphs: Unit 7: Nature and the Environment
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will explore the natural world — the weather and the climate, the beach and the coast, forests, mountains, and even our planet Earth. You will learn to talk about places you have visited using a new tense called the present perfect (“I have explored a forest”). You will describe nature with colourful words, ask good questions, and learn how to care for the environment. By the end, you will be ready to go on a nature adventure of your own, just like Ayo and Mirembe in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and describe places in nature: the beach, coast, forest, and mountain.
Talk about the weather, the climate, and the temperature.
Say what you have done using the present perfect: “I have seen the sea.”
Ask and answer questions like “Have you ever explored a forest?” — “Yes, I have.”
Use describing words to paint a clear picture: “a metal rock on the beach.”
Give instructions to care for the planet: “Protect nature.”
Read and enjoy the story “From Coast to Forest”.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few nature words each day.
Work through the nine short lessons in the Grammar file. Do one at a time — never all at once!
Read the Story file, “From Coast to Forest”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — Our Wonderful Nature
Read this short text:

Nature is all around us. The sunshine warms the land. Trees grow tall in the forest. Waves splash on the beach near the coast. High above, the mountain touches the clouds. All of this is our home — the planet Earth. We must take care of it.
After reading, answer:
Name three places in nature from the text.
What does the sunshine do for the land?
Why does the text say we must take care of the planet? What do you think?
Reading 2 — A Poem: “Nature Is Our Home”
Nature is our home,
It gives us water, trees, and air.
The sun, the sea, the mountain tall —
Let's take care, and share it all.
After reading, answer:
What does nature give us, in the poem?
What does the poem ask us to do?
You will write your own nature poem later. What part of nature do you like best?
Listening 1 — Getting Ready for the Trip
Listen to Teacher Lydia give instructions. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“Tomorrow we will explore the coast and the forest. Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see. And remember — protect the planet!”
What three things must the children bring or do?
Which two places will they explore?
Teacher Lydia gives instructions like “Bring water.” These are called imperatives. (See Grammar Lesson 5.)
Listening 2 — Have You Ever…?
Listen to two friends talking:
“Have you ever explored a forest?” asked Ayo.  “Yes, I have!” said one friend.  “No, I haven't,” said the other. “I have only seen pictures.”
What question does Ayo ask?
What are the two short answers you can hear? (See Grammar Lesson 3.)
Now answer for yourself: Have you ever explored a forest?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe a Place in Nature
Get ready to say three sentences about a place in nature:
“I like the ______ (beach / forest / mountain).”
“It is ______ and ______.” (describing words)
“There I can see ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place? Did you use two describing words? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Talk About the Weather
“Today the weather is ______.”
“The climate where I live is usually ______.”
“My favourite weather is ______ because ______.”
Speaking Game — Have You Ever…?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Ask a question: “Have you ever explored a ______ (forest / beach / mountain)?”
The other person answers with a short answer: “Yes, I have!” or “No, I haven't.”
If they say yes, ask: “What did you see?” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — Describe the Nature Near You
Write three or four sentences about the weather and nature near your home or village. Try to use these words: weather, sunshine, and one place word (beach, forest, or mountain).
Writing 2 — Three Things You Have Done
Write three sentences about things you have done in nature, using the present perfect:
“I have seen ______.”
“I have explored ______.”
“I have visited ______.”
Writing 3 — Your Own Nature Poem
Write your own version of “Nature Is Our Home”. Change the middle lines to the parts of nature you like:
Nature is our home, / It gives us ______ and ______, / The ______, the ______, the ______ — / Let's take care, and share it all.
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False (Nature Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A forest is a big area with many trees.
The coast is the land next to the sea.
Metal is a soft material that you can eat.
Earth is a planet.
Answers: 1. True   2. True   3. False (metal is a hard material used to make things)   4. True
Activity 2 — Match the Word to the Meaning
Match each word to its meaning.
climate     a) light and warmth from the sun
sunshine    b) the usual weather in a place
explore     c) to go and look at new places
temperature d) how hot or cold something is
Answers: 1–b   2–a   3–c   4–d
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (have / has)
I ______ explored a forest.
She ______ seen the mountain.
We ______ visited the beach.
He ______ climbed a hill.
Answers: 1. have   2. has   3. have   4. has
Activity 4 — Care for the Planet
Write two imperatives (instructions) that tell people how to care for nature. Start each one with a verb, for example: “Plant a tree.” Check your ideas with your AI tutor.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more ‘Have you ever…?’ questions?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine

Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 7: Nature and the Environment
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “From Coast to Forest”
From Coast to Forest
It was the first week of term, and Teacher Lydia had a big surprise. “We are going on a nature trip!” she said. The whole class cheered.
“We will explore the coast, play on the beach, and hike a mountain trail,” she said. Then she smiled. “Have you ever explored a forest?”
“Yes, I have!” said Ayo.
“No, I haven't,” said Mirembe. “I have only seen pictures.”
The next day, the class packed their bags. “Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see,” said Teacher Lydia. “And remember — protect the planet. Take only pictures, leave only footprints.”
Their first stop was the beach. The sunshine was bright, and the weather was hot. They felt the soft, warm sand and saw tiny crabs running near the waves.
“This is the coast,” said the teacher. “Look at the water and the wide sky.”
Ayo pointed to something shiny in the sand. “It's a rock — no, it's metal!”
“Touch it carefully,” said Teacher Lydia. “It may be sharp.” The children sat down and wrote a little poem together:
I see the waves,
I see the sand,
I hold a metal rock in my hand.
Later, they drove to the forest. The air felt cooler here. “What is the temperature?” asked the teacher.
“Maybe 22 degrees,” said Mirembe.
“That's right!” she said. “The climate is different here — more shade, and less wind.”
They walked along a path between the tall trees. “Have you ever been in a forest this big?” asked Ayo.
“No, I haven't,” said Mirembe. “It feels like another world.”
Suddenly, they saw something in the dirt — white and soft, like snow. “Is that ice?” asked Ayo.
“No, it is not snow,” said the teacher. “But once, water here froze during a very cold night.”
“Frozen water, here in Africa?” asked Mirembe, surprised.
“Yes,” the teacher smiled. “Even here, the weather can surprise us.”
They climbed higher and reached the mountain viewpoint. “Look down,” said Teacher Lydia. “You can see the beach and the forest from up here.” The wind blew gently across their faces.
“The air feels so fresh,” said Mirembe.
“That's natural energy,” said the teacher. “It comes from the sun and the wind.”
On the way back, they stopped near a pond. “What is all this white foam?” asked Ayo.
“It is a type of matter,” said Teacher Lydia. “Foam, water, rock, and air — they are all matter, just in different forms.”
Back at school, the students shared what they had learned. “I wrote about the sunshine,” said Ayo. “It helps the plants grow near the coast.”
“I wrote about the forest,” said Mirembe. “I have seen animals and trees I never knew before.”
The teacher asked, “Have you ever visited the beach or a mountain before this trip?” Some children said yes. Others said no. But now, they all had new memories.
Later, they made posters about nature. Mirembe's poster said:
Nature is our home.
It gives us water, trees, and air.
Let's take care — it's only fair.
That afternoon, Ayo wrote in his notebook: “The planet has so much to see. I have explored a forest, felt sunshine on the beach, and climbed a mountain. Nature is not just green — it is exciting.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What three places did the class visit on their nature trip?
What shiny thing did Ayo find in the sand on the beach?

Think and talk

Which place would you most like to visit — the beach, the forest, or the mountain? Why?
Mirembe said the forest “feels like another world.” Have you ever been somewhere that felt new and exciting?

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 8: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Eight grammar mini-lessons

This unit has eight small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! All the examples use maths and measuring words from this unit.
Lesson 1:  Present Simple and Imperatives
What it means and why it matters

The Present Simple tells about things that happen often or facts that are always true, like “The sun rises in the east.” An imperative is a command or instruction, like “Measure the height.” Both help us speak and write clearly, especially in maths.
How to form it
Present Simple: subject + verb (add s for he, she, it). “I count the shells.” / “She counts the shells.”
Imperative: just the verb, with no subject. “Count the numbers.” / “Measure the table.”
Negative imperative: use Don't. “Don't run in the hall.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He measure the desk.”  ✗
Fix: add s for he/she/it: “He measures the desk.”  ✓
Memory tip
For an imperative, just say the verb — you are the boss giving one clear order. For he, she, it in Present Simple, don't forget the s!
Practice 1
______ (Count) the numbers on the board. (imperative)
She ______ (measure) the rope every morning. (present simple)
______ (Measure) the height of your desk. (imperative)
We ______ (add) the prices at the market. (present simple)
Lesson 2:  ‘Have got’ for Ownership
What it means and why it matters
We use have got to talk about things we own or have. It helps us tell people about our belongings, like “I've got a ruler.”
How to form it
I / you / we / they have got (’ve got). “We have got ten bowls.”
He / she / it has got (’s got). “She has got a new ruler.”
Negative: haven't got / hasn't got. “They haven't got any shells.”
Question: Have you got …? “Have you got a meter stick?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He have got a bicycle.”  ✗
Fix: use has got for he/she/it: “He has got a bicycle.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of ‘have got’ like a treasure chest — you open it to show what you own. He, she, it → has got.
Practice 2
I ______ (have got / haven't got) a new maths book. I love it!
She ______ (has got / hasn't got) a ruler, so she cannot draw a straight line.
We ______ (have got / haven't got) a lot of homework tonight.
______ you ______ (Have / got) a meter stick?
Lesson 3:  Can / Can't for Ability
What it means and why it matters
We use can to say what we are able to do, and can't for what we are not able to do. It helps us talk about our skills, like “I can measure the distance.”
How to form it
subject + can + base verb. “You can measure the distance.”
subject + can't + base verb. “You can't subtract letters.”
Question: Can + subject + base verb? “Can you count to a million?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I can swimming.” / “I can measuring.”  ✗
Fix: use the base verb after can: “I can swim.” / “I can measure it.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “can” as a superhero cape. “I can” = cape on, you have the power. “I can't” = cape off.
Practice 3
I ______ (can / can't) count to one hundred. It is easy!
She ______ (can / can't) reach the top shelf; she is too short.
______ (Can / Can't) you measure this shell for me, please?
We ______ (can / can't) subtract letters — only numbers!
Lesson 4:  Demonstratives (This / That)
What it means and why it matters
This and that point to things. We use this for something near us and that for something farther away. This makes it clear which thing we mean.
How to form it
This (near, one thing). “This is a straight line.”
That (far, one thing). “That pattern is complex.”
These (near, many) / those (far, many). “These are my rulers.” / “Those are your shells.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “That is my pencil,” when the pencil is in your hand.  ✗
Fix: it is near you, so use this: “This is my pencil.”  ✓
Memory tip
Say it like a rhyme: “This is near, that is clear (over there)!”
Practice 4
______ (This / That) shell in my hand is very small.
______ (This / That) tree over there is very tall.
Look at ______ (this / that) straight line I drew here!
Do you see ______ (this / those) mountains far in the distance?
Lesson 5:  Future with ‘Will’
What it means and why it matters
We use will to talk about the future — promises, plans, and predictions. It tells what has not happened yet, like “I will measure the meter tomorrow.”
How to form it
subject + will + base verb. “I will multiply the numbers.”
Negative: will not (won't). “I won't forget the answer.”
Question: Will + subject + base verb? “Will you measure it tomorrow?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I will multiplied the numbers.”  ✗
Fix: use the base verb after will: “I will multiply the numbers.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of “will” as a magic word for time travel to tomorrow. “I will” is your ticket to the future!
Practice 5
I ______ (read) a new maths book next week.
They ______ (measure) the classroom tomorrow.
She ______ (count) the shells later.
We ______ (not / forget) to check the pattern.
Lesson 6:  Short Future Responses
What it means and why it matters
Short future responses let us answer quickly about the future. We say I will to agree, and I won't to say no. They make our answers clear and confident.
How to form it
To say yes to a future action: “I will!”
To say no: “I won't.” (won't = will not)
You can use other subjects too: “She will.” / “They won't.”
A common mistake
Mistake: writing the negative as “I not will.”  ✗
Fix: the short negative is I won't: “I won't.”  ✓
Memory tip
“Will” = a promise (Yes!). “Won't” = no promise (No!).
Practice 6
Answer with “I will” or “I won't”:
A: “Will you help me measure the desk?”  B: “______, I'd love to!”
A: “Will you count all the way to a million?”  B: “______, that would take too long!”
A: “Will you check the pattern again?”  B: “______, good idea.”
Lesson 7:  Question Forms
What it means and why it matters
Questions help us find information. Good learners ask lots of questions! There are Yes/No questions and Wh- questions (what, where, when, why, who, how).
How to form it
Yes/No: start with a helping verb. “Can you count?” / “Is the line straight?”
Wh-: start with a question word. “What is the distance?” / “How many shells are there?”
Suggestions: use Shall. “Shall I measure the size?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You can play?” (subject and verb not swapped)  ✗
Fix: swap them for a question: “Can you play?”  ✓
Memory tip
For a question, flip the subject and the helping verb: “You are” → “Are you?”
Practice 7
Write the missing question word or helping word:
______ is the distance from the door to the window? (asks about a thing)
______ you measure this shell for me? (Yes/No, about ability)
______ many bowls are on the table? (asks about number)
______ we check our pattern again? (a suggestion)
Lesson 8:  Cause and Effect with ‘Because’
What it means and why it matters

We use because to explain WHY something happens. It joins the effect (what happened) with the cause (the reason). This makes our writing stronger, like “I added a number because the pattern was not complete.”
How to form it
effect + because + cause. “I checked again because the answer looked wrong.”
You can also start with the cause: “Because it was raining, we stayed inside.”
A common mistake
Mistake: leaving out the reason: “I added a number.” (Why?)  ✗
Fix: add the reason with because: “I added a number because the pattern was not complete.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of because as the glue that sticks two ideas together: what happened + why.
Practice 8
Finish each sentence with a reason using “because”:
We worked as a team ______.
I used multiplication ______.
Afi could not measure the shell ______.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Simple / Imperatives)
1. Count   2. measures   3. Measure   4. add
Practice 2 (Have got)
1. have got   2. hasn't got   3. have got   4. Have … got
Practice 3 (Can / Can't)
1. can   2. can't   3. Can   4. can't
Practice 4 (This / That)
1. This   2. That   3. this   4. those
Practice 5 (Future with Will)
1. will read   2. will measure   3. will count   4. will not forget (won't forget)
Practice 6 (Short Future Responses)
1. I will   2. I won't   3. I will
Practice 7 (Question Forms)
1. What   2. Can   3. How   4. Shall
Practice 8 (Because) — example answers
We worked as a team because we learn more together.
I used multiplication because it is faster than adding the same number many times.
Afi could not measure the shell because it was too small.
