paragraphs: Unit 8: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will use English to talk about maths in real life. You will count and name big numbers, all the way up to a million. You will learn the words for addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. You will measure things around you — their size, height, weight, and the distance between them — using words like meter and centimeter. You will also look for patterns and straight lines. Maths is everywhere: in the market, in the kitchen, and on the way to the masjid. By the end, you will be ready to solve a number puzzle, just like Kwame and Afi do in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and say big numbers, up to a million.
Use the maths words addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
Measure the size, height, weight, and distance of things around you.
Talk about straight lines and repeating patterns.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Measure the height.”).
Say what you own with have got, and what you can do with can / can't.
Talk about the future with will, and explain reasons using because.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few maths words each day.
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “The Mystery of the Million Shells”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — Maths Is Everywhere
Read this short text:

Numbers are all around us. When Amina helps her mother at the market, she uses addition to add the prices. When they share the dates with four cousins, they use division. Amina measures the size of the flour bag and checks its weight. On the way home, she counts her steps to find the distance to their door. Maths helps us every day!
After reading, answer:
Which maths word does Amina use to add the prices at the market?
What does Amina do when she shares the dates with four cousins?
Name two things Amina measures.
Where do YOU use maths in your day? Give one example.
Reading 2 — A Pattern Poem
Read this poem out loud. Listen for the pattern:
One, two, buckle my shoe,
Three, four, count some more,
Five, six, shapes and sticks,
Straight and round, patterns found!
After reading, answer:
Which numbers can you find in the poem? Say them in order.
The poem talks about a pattern. What repeats in the poem?
Can you make one more line that rhymes? Try: “Seven, eight, ______.”
Listening 1 — The Measuring Challenge
Listen to Teacher Halima give the class a challenge. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“Good morning, class! Today we will measure three things. First, measure the height of your desk. Next, measure the distance from the door to the window. Then, find the weight of your school bag. Write each number in your book. You can use a meter stick. Ready? Begin!”
How many things does the class measure?
What is the FIRST thing Teacher Halima asks them to measure?
What tool can the students use?
Notice: Teacher Halima says “Measure the height.” This is an imperative — a clear instruction. (See Grammar Lesson 1.)
Listening 2 — Numbers Big and Small
Listen and say each number after your AI tutor:
“Ten. One hundred. One thousand. Ten thousand. One hundred thousand. One million! A million is a very big number — it is one thousand thousands.”
Which is the biggest number in the list?
How many is a million? (Hint: one thousand thousands.)
Which number comes just before one million in the list?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Measure and Tell
Choose something in your home. Measure it, then say:
“This is a ______. Its height is about ______.”
“Its weight is about ______.”
“The distance from it to the door is about ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use a measuring word (meter, centimeter, kilo)? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Maths in the Market
Imagine you are shopping. Practise saying:
“I will buy three bags. (using will)”
“I've got ten shillings. (using have got)”
“Can you measure the flour for me, please? (using can)”
Speaking Game — Guess My Pattern
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says the start of a pattern: “Two, four, six, ______?”
The other person guesses the next number and says the rule: “Eight! You are adding two.”
Try shape patterns too: “circle, square, circle, square, ______?” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — How-To Instructions
Write four steps to measure the height of your table. Use imperatives (start each step with a verb):
Step 1: Put the meter stick ______.
Step 4: Write down the ______.
Writing 2 — My Maths Report
Write three sentences using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one maths fact you know. For example:
“I measured the distance from my bed to the door. I found a pattern in the tiles. One fact I know: 5 × 5 = 25.”
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Maths Word
Match each word to the right meaning. Write the letter.
addition        (a) taking one number away from another
subtraction  (b) putting numbers together to get a total
multiplication  (c) splitting into equal parts
division        (d) adding the same number many times
Answers: 1. b   2. a   3. d   4. c
Activity 2 — True or False (Number Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A million is one thousand thousands.
A straight line is bent and curved.
We use a meter to measure length.
In subtraction, 6 minus 2 equals 5.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (a straight line is NOT bent or curved)   3. True   4. False (6 − 2 = 4)
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
Choose the best word: height, weight, distance, pattern.
The ______ of the tree is very tall.
The ______ of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
The ______ from school to my house is short.
The red-blue-red-blue design is a colour ______.
Answers: 1. height   2. weight   3. distance   4. pattern
Activity 4 — Count and Multiply
There are 25 shells in one bowl. There are 10 bowls.
How many shells are there in total? (Use multiplication: 25 × 10)
If you take away 5 shells, how many are left? (Use subtraction.)
Answers: 1. 250 shells   2. 245 shells
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand multiplication. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this big number? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my maths report and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more measuring questions?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and say big numbers up to a million.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division words.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can measure size, height, weight, and distance.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about straight lines and patterns.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give an instruction using an imperative.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use “have got”, “can/can't”, and “will”.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 9
Next you will study Unit 9: “Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination”. Get ready:
Make sure you know all the Unit 8 words on your Word Wall.
Start noticing feeling words in English: happy, worried, excited, proud.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 9? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — maths and English go together! Keep counting, measuring, and finding patterns every day. 🌟

Start with a capital letter.   ·   End with a full stop.
Write numbers clearly.   ·   Use “because” to give a reason.
Read your sentence again to check it makes sense.

“Can you check my maths report and my spelling?”

🕑  About 30–45 minutes a day
Morning: review your vocabulary words and say five big numbers out loud.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 8: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Mystery of the Million Shells”
The Mystery of the Million Shells
It was a hot afternoon in Cape Coast. The sea breeze blew through the windows of Class 3B.
“Today,” said Teacher Esi, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.

Kwame looked at the table. There were seashells, string, rulers, and bowls. “Each team will measure the size, weight, and distance between the shells,” said Teacher Esi. “Use your eyes, your hands, and your maths.”
“What is the first step?” asked Afi.
“Measure the height of your tallest shell,” said the teacher.
Kwame's team got to work. “This is a straight shell,” said Afi. “That one is curved.”
“This shell is 2 meters from the bowl,” said Kwame. “Let's write that down,” said Afi. The teams wrote their numbers in their books:
Height: 8 cm.   Distance: 2 meters.   Weight: 150 grams.
“Let's do some addition and subtraction,” said Kwame. Afi smiled. “And don't forget multiplication and division!”
Suddenly, Afi pointed at the board. “There's a clue!” she shouted. It read: “There are more than 500 shells. Fewer than a million. Use a pattern. Count carefully.”
Kwame counted the shells in one bowl. “There are 25 in this one. And there are 10 bowls.”
“25 times 10 is …?” Afi asked. “Multiplication!” said Kwame. “That's 250.”
“And what if we add the shells on the table?” Afi asked. Kwame replied, “Then it's over 300. Maybe 400.” “Still far from a million,” Afi laughed.

“Can you measure this shell?” Kwame asked. “I can't,” Afi said. “It's too small, and the line isn't straight.” “Use the other ruler,” said Kwame. “It has better units.” “Good idea,” Afi said. “I will do it now.”
Teacher Esi walked by. “These numbers are correct. But remember, maths is not only about numbers. It is about facts and smart thinking.”
“Shall we check our pattern again?” Afi asked. “Yes,” said Kwame. “Let's see if our division is equal.”

Later that day, each team shared their answers. Afi stood up and said: “We used multiplication and addition. We counted by fives. We measured distance in meters and height in centimeters. Our total number of shells is 375.”
Teacher Esi smiled. “You all did well. The answer is … 375 shells. Afi's team found the right pattern.” The class clapped.
Kwame whispered, “Teamwork is good because we learn more together.”

The teacher gave a final task. “Write a report using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one new maths fact.” Kwame wrote: “We measured the distance from the bowl. We found a pattern in the numbers. One fact I learned: 25 × 10 = 250.”
As they packed up, Afi said, “I've got a new idea for next week. Let's count the school chairs.” Kwame laughed. “Only if we stop before a million!”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What was the challenge that Teacher Esi gave the class?
What is the first thing the teams had to measure?
How did Kwame find that there were 250 shells in the bowls? Which operation did he use?
What was the final total number of shells?
Teacher Esi said maths is not only about numbers. What else is it about?
Think and talk
Kwame said, “Teamwork is good because we learn more together.” Do you agree? Why?
Afi could not measure the tiny shell with one ruler. What did she do instead? What do you do when something is hard?
Where do YOU use numbers, measuring, or patterns in your own day?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 8 words and say the sentence they are in:
number
million
pattern
measure
height
distance
weight
multiplication
division
fact
Now You Try:  Writing
Write your own short maths report, like Kwame's. Use these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one maths fact you know.
Imagine your class does a counting challenge. Write three sentences about what you would count and how you would use multiplication or addition.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 9: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Grammar mini-lessons

This unit has several small grammar lessons. They help you talk about ideas, feelings, and imagination. Do one or two lessons at a time. For each lesson: read what it means, look at how to make it, study the mistake (✗) and the fix (✓), remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the examples out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Abstract Nouns
What it means and why it matters

An abstract noun is a word for something you cannot see, touch, or hold — like a feeling, a quality, or an idea. Words like idea, experience, sadness, and kindness are abstract nouns. They let you talk about what is inside your mind and heart.
How to use it
Name the feeling or idea:  “She shared an idea.”
Add a describing word to make it stronger:  “She shared a brilliant idea.”
More examples:  “I had a strange experience.”  ·  “His kindness made a difference.”
A common mistake
Mistake: forgetting that a feeling word is a noun. “She is very sad feeling.”  ✗
Fix: use the abstract noun: “She felt great sadness.”  ✓
Memory tip
Abstract nouns are like clouds — you cannot touch them, but you can still see their shape. Ask yourself: “Can I touch it?” If not, it is probably an abstract noun.
Practice 1
Fill in each blank with an abstract noun from the word bank: courage, happiness, idea, sadness.
His ______ helped him face his fears.
She felt pure ______ when she got the good news.
The teacher praised the student's brilliant ______.
He felt deep ______ when his friend moved away.
Lesson 2:  Modal Verbs (can, will, should, must)
What it means and why it matters

Modal verbs are little helper verbs that tell us what is possible, what we are able to do, what we are allowed to do, or what we plan to do. They are important because they add meaning to our sentences.
How to form it
Use this pattern:  subject + modal verb + base verb (the plain verb, with no “s” and no “to”).
can → able to:  “I can imagine a better world.”
will → a future plan:  “We will share more ideas.”
should → a good idea:  “We should help the new student.”
must → something you have to do:  “You must listen carefully.”
A common mistake
Mistake: adding “s” to the modal or the verb. “He cans swim.” / “She will swims.”  ✗
Fix: modals never change, and the verb stays plain: “He can swim.” / “She will swim.”  ✓
Memory tip
Modal verbs are “helpers”. A helper is always followed by the plain verb — never “to”, never an extra “s”.
Practice 2
Choose the best modal verb.
You ______ (can / must) go to bed early tonight — it is a rule.
We ______ (could / should) try to help the new student.
One day, people ______ (will / must) share more ideas.
______ (Can / Should) you imagine a world with no sadness?
Lesson 3:  Imperatives for Instructions
What it means and why it matters

An imperative is a sentence that gives an instruction, a command, or a friendly request. We use imperatives to tell someone what to do in a clear, direct way — like in a group discussion or a list of steps.
How to form it
Start with the plain verb. You do not need “you”:  “Allow others to speak.”
Add details to make it clear:  “Suggest a helpful idea for the project.”
For a “do not” command, start with Don't:  “Don't interrupt when someone is talking.”
To be polite, add please:  “Please listen carefully.”
A common mistake
Mistake: adding “you” or using the wrong verb form. “You allowing others to speak.”  ✗
Fix: just start with the plain verb: “Allow others to speak.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of an imperative as a friendly order to a friend: clear and helpful, but never bossy.
Practice 3
Write the correct imperative using the verb in brackets.
______ (help) your classmates when they ask.
______ (not interrupt) when someone else is talking.
______ (share) your ideas with the group.
______ (be) respectful to everyone.
Lesson 4:  Countable and Uncountable Nouns
What it means and why it matters

Countable nouns are things you can count, like ideas — one idea, two ideas. Uncountable nouns cannot be counted one by one, like sadness or water. Knowing the difference helps you choose the right words for “how much” and “how many”.
How to form it
Countable: use a / an, numbers, or many:  “a reason”, “three ideas”, “many choices”.
Uncountable: use some or much, and no “a/an”:  “some water”, “much experience”.
Countable nouns can be plural (add s):  one idea → two ideas. Uncountable nouns do not change.
A common mistake
Mistake: “I have a water.” / “She has much ideas.”  ✗
Fix: “I have some water.” / “She has many ideas.”  ✓
Memory tip
Countable nouns you can drop into a bag and count one by one. Uncountable nouns are like liquids or feelings — you cannot split them into single pieces.
Practice 4
Choose the correct words.
I have ______ (a reason / some reasons) to be happy.
There is ______ (many water / some water) in the glass.
She has ______ (three experiences / much experience) with drawing.
Lesson 5:  Reported Speech
What it means and why it matters

Reported speech is how we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words. Instead of “He said, ‘I feel hopeless,’” we say, “He said he felt hopeless.” This lets you share other people's ideas and feelings.
How to form it
Start with a reporting verb like said or told.
Change the pronoun to fit: “I” becomes “he” or “she”.
Change the verb to the past: “feel” becomes “felt”, “can” becomes “could”, “will” becomes “would”.
Take away the quotation marks.
A common mistake
Mistake: keeping the present tense and the quote marks. “She said, ‘My choice is thoughtful.’”  ✗
Fix: “She said her choice was thoughtful.”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember three steps: change the pronoun, change the tense, and drop the quotes.
Practice 5
Rewrite each sentence in reported speech.
Direct: “I love ice cream.”  →  Reported: He said ______.
Direct: “She will come to the game.”  →  Reported: She said ______.
Direct: “We are happy.”  →  Reported: They said ______.
Lesson 6:  Conjunctions (and, but, or, so, because)
What it means and why it matters
Conjunctions are joining words. They glue two ideas together so your sentences flow. The most useful ones are and, but, or, so, and because.
How to use it
and adds:  “I like thinking and dreaming.”
but shows a difference:  “I like the idea, but it is hard.”
or offers a choice:  “Do you want juice or milk?”
so shows a result:  “It was raining, so we stayed inside.”
because gives a reason:  “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.”
A common mistake
Mistake: using the wrong joining word. “I enjoy thinking, but it helps me learn.”  ✗
Fix: “but” shows a difference, not a reason. Use because: “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.”  ✓
Memory tip
Conjunctions are glue for your ideas. Pick the glue that matches: adding, contrasting, choosing, result, or reason.
Practice 6
Fill in each blank with: and, but, or, so, or because.
I like video games, ______ my brother prefers board games.
We wanted to go to the park, ______ it was too hot.
He studied all night, ______ he wanted to pass the test.
She enjoys painting ______ it helps her relax.
Lesson 7:  Simple Past in Stories
What it means and why it matters
The simple past tense tells about things that already happened. We use it all the time in stories, so the reader knows the actions are finished.
How to form it
Most verbs add -ed:  imagine → imagined  ·  allow → allowed  ·  play → played.
Some verbs are irregular and change shape:  go → went  ·  see → saw  ·  have → had.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She imagine a better world.” / “He goed to the store.”  ✗
Fix: “She imagined a better world.” / “He went to the store.”  ✓
Memory tip
Most verbs just need -ed. A few “special” (irregular) verbs must be learned by heart — like went, saw, and had.
Practice 7
Write the simple past of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday, she ______ (imagine) a magical land.
Last week, he ______ (allow) his friend to join the game.
They ______ (see) a beautiful sunset.
Lesson 8:  The Future with “Will”
What it means and why it matters
We use will to talk about the future — our plans, promises, and predictions. It is like making a little promise about what is going to happen.
How to form it
Pattern:  subject + will + plain verb:  “I will play.”  ·  “She will read a book.”
Negative: add not (won't):  “I will not forget.”
Question: put will first:  “Will you come to the party?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I will going.” / “You will play?”  ✗
Fix: use the plain verb, and put “will” first in questions: “I will go.” / “Will you play?”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of will as a promise about the future. After the promise, always use the plain verb.
Practice 8
Complete each sentence with “will” and the verb in brackets.
She ______ (play) the piano at the concert.
They ______ (not / go) to the beach tomorrow.
I ______ (help) you with your homework.
Lesson 9:  Opinion Questions
What it means and why it matters
Opinion questions ask what someone thinks or feels. They help us learn about each other and share our own ideas. This unit uses them a lot, like “What do you believe?”
How to form it
Use What for an open opinion:  “What do you think about school?”
Use Is / Are for a yes/no opinion:  “Is sadness always a bad thing?”
Use Do for beliefs:  “Do you believe in kindness?”
A common mistake
Mistake: leaving out the question word. “You like the idea?”  ✗
Fix: start with a question word: “Do you like the idea?”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember the start words: What · Is · Should · Do. Begin your opinion question with one of them.
Practice 9
Choose the correct question word.
______ (What / Where) do you think about summer holidays?
______ (Is / Are) homework always necessary?
______ (Do / Did) you believe that everyone should be allowed to play?
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Abstract Nouns)
1. courage   2. happiness   3. idea   4. sadness
Practice 2 (Modal Verbs)
1. must   2. should   3. will   4. Can
Practice 3 (Imperatives)
1. Help   2. Don't interrupt   3. Share   4. Be
Practice 4 (Countable / Uncountable)
1. a reason   2. some water   3. much experience
Practice 5 (Reported Speech)
He said he loved ice cream.
She said she would come to the game.
They said they were happy.
Practice 6 (Conjunctions)
1. but   2. but   3. because   4. because
Practice 7 (Simple Past)
1. imagined   2. allowed   3. saw
Practice 8 (Future with “Will”)
1. will play   2. will not (won't) go   3. will help
Practice 9 (Opinion Questions)
1. What   2. Is   3. Do

paragraphs: Unit 9: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to the last unit of Year 3! This unit is all about the world inside your head — your thinking, your feelings, and your imagination. You will learn words for ideas and emotions, like idea, sense, reason, sadness, and experience. You will learn how to imagine new things, how to make a choice, and how to suggest ideas kindly to your friends. You will read about children in a coastal East African town who fill a special Box of Ideas with their dreams and hopes. By the end, you will be able to talk about what you think and feel, and use your imagination to write your own ideas down.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use abstract nouns like idea, reason, experience, and sadness to talk about thoughts and feelings.
Say what you imagine and describe pictures in your mind.
Give a reason for something using because.
Use modal verbs like can and will to talk about what is possible and what you plan to do.
Suggest ideas and allow others to speak in a group.
Tell someone what another person said, using reported speech.
Answer opinion questions such as “What do you believe?”
Write about a feeling, a memory, or something you imagine.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few feeling-and-thinking words each day.
Work through the mini-lessons in the Grammar file (abstract nouns, modal verbs, imperatives, and more).
Read the Story file, “The Box of Ideas”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment, then celebrate — you will have finished Year 3!
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — The Box of Ideas at School
Read this short text out loud:

Our teacher brought a bright box to class. “This is a Box of Ideas,” she said. “Every child will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.” My friend Idris had a good idea, but he was not sure about its purpose. “Imagine something better,” I told him. So he closed his eyes and thought. His idea was about flying over the sea. It was a thoughtful and sincere idea, and the whole class smiled.
After reading, answer:
What did the teacher bring to class? What can the children put inside it?
Why was Idris not sure about his idea at first?
Find one thoughtful thing and one sincere thing in the text.
What is one idea YOU would put in the Box of Ideas? Say your reason.
Reading 2 — Feelings Are Not Bad or Good
Read this short text out loud:

Every day we feel many things. Sometimes we feel joy, and sometimes we feel sadness. Sadness is not a bad thing — it is a feeling, like rain is a kind of weather. When Aisha felt hopeless because her cat was lost, she talked to her mother. Her mother listened with a sincere heart. “It is good to share how you feel,” she said. “When we talk about sadness, we help each other. That is a kind and thoughtful thing to do.” Soon, Aisha felt hopeful again.
After reading, answer:
What two feelings does the text talk about?
Why did Aisha feel hopeless? Who did she talk to?
What does the text say we should do when we feel sadness? Do you agree?
Listening 1 — A Group Discussion
Listen to this classroom talk. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
Teacher: “Today we plan our class garden. Please suggest an idea, and allow everyone to speak.”
Yusuf: “I suggest we grow tomatoes, because we can eat them at lunch.”
Hodan: “That is a good idea. I think we should grow flowers too, so the garden looks beautiful.”
Teacher: “Thank you both. Those are thoughtful choices.”
What are the children planning to make?
What did Yusuf suggest? What reason did he give?
The teacher used two commands (imperatives). What did she ask the class to do? (See Grammar Lesson 3.)
Listening 2 — What Idris Said
Listen, then practise telling the story to someone else.
Idris said, “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly.” Later, his friend told her mother: “Idris said he could imagine flying. He said it made him feel free.”
First Idris spoke for himself. Then his friend told her mother. How did the words change?
Why do the words “I” and “can” change to “he” and “could”? (See Grammar Lesson 5: Reported Speech.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Share an Idea
Get ready to say your idea and your reason:
“I have an idea. I suggest we ______.”
“The reason is that ______.”
“What do you think about my idea?”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say your idea clearly? Did you give a reason with because? Did you invite the other person to answer?
Speaking 2 — Talk About Your Feelings
Practise talking about a feeling. It is brave and healthy to share how you feel.
“Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
“When I feel sadness, I like to ______.”
“Something that makes me feel hopeful is ______.”
Speaking Game — Imagine It!
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says: “Imagine you can ______ (fly / talk to animals / be invisible).”
The other person answers: “If I could ______, I would ______, because ______.”
Then swap turns. Try to use a new idea each time!
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — My Idea for the Box
Imagine you can add one idea to the Box of Ideas. Write three or four sentences about your dream, memory, or hope. Try to use the words idea, imagine, and because.
Start like this:  “My idea is ______. I imagine ______. This is important to me because ______.”
Writing 2 — A Time I Used My Imagination
Write about a time you used your imagination to solve a problem or feel better. What did you imagine? How did it help you? Use the simple past tense (imagined, allowed, felt).
Writing 3 — My Opinion
Answer this opinion question in three sentences: “Is it good to share our feelings, even our sadness?” Say what you believe and give a reason.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False (Feelings and Ideas)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
An idea is a thought or plan in your mind.
Sadness is a feeling we should never talk about.
To imagine means to picture something in your mind.
A reason tells us why something happens.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (it is healthy and kind to share sadness)   3. True   4. True
Activity 2 — Choose the Feeling Word
Choose the best word: sincere, thoughtful, hopeless, or enjoyable.
The class trip to the sea was very ______. We had so much fun.
She gave a ______ apology, straight from her heart.
Bringing a gift for a sick friend is a ______ thing to do.
When I could not find my shoe, I felt ______.
Answers: 1. enjoyable   2. sincere   3. thoughtful   4. hopeless
Activity 3 — Add the Reason
Finish each sentence with a reason, using because.
I like to imagine, because ______.
I suggest we help the new student, because ______.
Sadness is not a bad thing, because ______.
Answers will be different for everyone. Ask your AI tutor to check that your reason makes sense.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more sentences with abstract nouns?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use abstract nouns like idea, reason, and sadness.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what I imagine.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give a reason using “because”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use “can” and “will” to talk about the future.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can suggest an idea and let others speak.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can tell someone what another person said.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can answer an opinion question.   Yes □   Not yet □
Congratulations — You Finished Year 3!

You have reached the end of the very last unit of Year 3. That is a wonderful achievement! Think back to everything you can now do in English: you can read stories, ask and answer questions, describe people and places, and now you can even talk about your ideas, feelings, and imagination.
Look back at your Word Walls from every unit. Say how many words you have learned this year — be proud!
Keep reading, speaking, and writing a little in English every day, even over your break.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Year 4? Can you help me get ready?”
Show your teacher your favourite piece of writing from this year and tell them why you like it.
You worked hard all year, and you did it one small step at a time. Take a moment to celebrate, then get ready for an exciting new adventure in Year 4. Well done, and mabrook! 🌟

“Can you help me talk about a feeling in a kind way?”

✍️  Writing rules to remember
Start every sentence with a capital letter.   ·   End with a full stop, a question mark, or an exclamation mark.
Use because to join a sentence to its reason.   ·   Use and or but to join two ideas.
Read your writing back out loud. Does it make sense? Fix anything that does not.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me add a reason with ‘because’?”
“Can you help me make my imagination story more exciting?”

Morning: review your vocabulary words and use two of them in a sentence out loud.
Afternoon: do one grammar mini-lesson OR one skill activity from this file.
Evening: read a little of “The Box of Ideas” and talk to your AI tutor about a feeling or idea.
Twice a week: bring your recordings and writing to your teacher session.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 9: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Box of Ideas”
The Box of Ideas
At a school near the sea, the teacher, Macallimad Zainab, stood at the front of the class holding a bright, colourful box. The children leaned forward to look.
“This,” she said, “is our Box of Ideas. Each of you will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.”
“Why?” asked Idris.
“Because ideas help us grow,” the teacher said. “They come from feeling, thinking, and listening. Every idea has a reason.”
The next day, Idris sat quietly under a tree in the schoolyard. He wrote something on a small paper and folded it. Then he looked around, but he did not smile.
“What is the matter?” asked his classmate, Faduma.
“I don't think my idea is good,” said Idris. “I don't even know its purpose.”
“Try again,” she said kindly. “Imagine something better.”
Faduma showed him what she had written: “One day I will write music with the sense of rain and drums. It will be enjoyable, like dancing at the beach.”
When Idris placed his paper in the box, he still felt unsure.
Later, the whole class sat together in a circle. The teacher opened the box and read the first paper: “I want a garden where children feel peace.”
“What a lovely choice,” said Faduma. “That is so thoughtful.”
The teacher read another: “I would allow everyone to play, no matter what.”
“That shows a kind purpose,” said the teacher, smiling.
Then she read one more: “I once felt hopeless when my father was ill. But he got better, and now I plant trees with him.” The room went very quiet.
“That is very sincere,” the teacher said softly.
Faduma nodded. “That story has a strong reason.”

At last the teacher reached Idris's paper. She read: “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly. I would fly over the sea and wave at my grandfather's house. I don't know what it means. But I like the feeling.”
The whole class smiled. Faduma clapped softly. “That is the best idea yet,” she said.
After class, the teacher said, “Idris, you shared something thoughtful. That is what imagination is for.” Idris smiled at last.
That evening, Faduma and Idris walked home together along the shore. “I suggest we make a music box next,” said Faduma.
“What would it play?” asked Idris.
“Whatever we feel,” she said.
The next morning, the teacher asked the class a question: “What do you believe — should we share our ideas, even when we feel sadness?”
“Yes,” said Idris. “Sadness can help others feel understood.”
The teacher smiled. “Then you have all learned something important.”

The children wrote in their journals. Idris wrote: “I learned that an experience does not need to be big to have a reason. I will keep writing and imagining.” Faduma wrote: “I will try to be more sincere when I share.”
At the end of the week, the box was full. “Now we will seal it and open it again next year,” the teacher said.
“Why?” asked Idris again.
“Because then you will see how your ideas and choices have grown.”
As the children walked out into the warm afternoon, Idris whispered, “I used to feel unsure. Now I believe that imagination is a kind of flying.”
Faduma laughed. “And we can fly anytime,” she said, “with a good idea.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What did the teacher bring to class, and what did she ask each child to put inside it?
Why did Idris feel unsure about his idea at first? Who helped him?
What was written on the paper that made the whole room go quiet?
What was Idris's own idea? How did the class feel about it?
Why does the teacher want to seal the box and open it next year?
Think and talk
How did Idris feel at the start of the story? How did he feel at the end? What changed him?
Idris said, “Sadness can help others feel understood.” What do you think he meant? Do you agree?
If you could add one idea to the Box of Ideas, what would it be? What is your reason?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 9 words and say the sentence they are in:
idea
reason
purpose
imagine
sense
choice
thoughtful
sincere
hopeless
sadness
experience
suggest
allow
enjoyable
Now You Try:  Writing
Write your own paper for the Box of Ideas — a dream, a memory, or a hope. Write three or four sentences. Try to use the words idea, imagine, and because.
Idris imagined he could fly. Write about something YOU like to imagine. Where would you go? How would it feel?
Draw the Box of Ideas. Around it, write three ideas that different children might put inside.

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 9: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination
Year 3 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 9. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the quick practice. Many of these words are about thoughts and feelings — things you cannot see or touch, but that live in your mind and heart. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Thinking Words
Practise these words
Say one idea you have. Then say: “The reason is ______.”
Close your eyes and imagine a place. Say three things you can see there.
Finish the sentence: “I had to make a choice between ______ and ______.”
Group 2: Feeling Words
Practise these words
Finish the sentence: “Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
Tell about an enjoyable experience you have had. Use both words in one sentence.
Name one thoughtful thing a friend has done for you.
Group 3: Sharing and Talking Words
Practise these words
Suggest an idea to a friend: “I suggest we ______.”
Finish the sentence: “My teacher allows us to ______.”
Say one thing you believe, and give a reason.
Word Families: From Feelings to Words
Many feeling words come from smaller words. Notice how the endings change the word:
sad → sadness  ·  happy → happiness  ·  kind → kindness
hope → hopeless (without hope)  ·  enjoy → enjoyable (able to be enjoyed)
thought → thoughtful (full of thought for others)
Say each pair out loud. Can you hear how the little word grows into a bigger one?

tables: A thought or plan in your mind.
I have an idea for a fun game we can play.
To make a picture of something in your mind.
I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.
The cause of something, or why it is done.
I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.
The reason why something is made or done.
The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.
A decision between two or more things.
You can have a cookie or an apple; it is your choice.
A way to feel or understand something.
I had a sense that it might rain soon.

The feeling you have when you are unhappy.

Something that happens to you that you remember.

Honest and truly meaning what you say.
She gave me a sincere smile when I helped her.
Kind and caring about other people.
She gave her friend a gift because she was thoughtful.
Feeling like nothing will get better.
I felt hopeless when I could not find my lost toy.
